meta.description: A machine-readable knowledge graph of formal learning theory. 142 nodes (133 kernel). 260 edges with 13 typed, invertible relation types. Formal definitions are neutral mathematical statements.
meta.status: v1.0 — Release. Single authoritative schema with 13 typed relations.

scope.primary: Binary classification formal learning theory (PAC, online, Gold, universal, PAC-Bayes, stability, info-theoretic)
included_extensions: Multiclass: Natarajan dimension, DS dimension (scope_note on each node)
Real-valued: pseudodimension, fat-shattering dimension (scope_note on each node)
Margin theory (scope_note: bridges binary VCdim with practical neural net generalization)
Bayesian: PAC-Bayes, posterior consistency, Gibbs posterior
Granger causality (application-level only, no new types)
explicit_exclusions: Bandits (EXP3/UCB — different optimization framework, not classification)

instance_of.semantics: X is a specific instance/specialization of Y.

characterizes.semantics: X ↔ Y: equivalence under stated conditions.

upper_bounds.semantics: X provides upper bound on Y.

lower_bounds.semantics: X provides lower bound on Y.

requires_assumption.semantics: X holds only if Y is assumed.

strictly_stronger.semantics: X ⊋ Y with known witness for separation.

measures.semantics: Complexity measure X quantifies property of Y.

does_not_imply.semantics: X does NOT imply Y, with witness.

used_in_proof.semantics: Theorem X uses concept Y in its statement or proof. Distinct from defined_using (definitional prerequisite).

restricts.semantics: X generalizes Y by removing a constraint. Y = X + additional restriction. No new grammar needed.

extends_grammar.semantics: X generalizes Y but required inventing new concepts/primitives not present in Y. Grammar expansion.

json_schema.description: SOLE authoritative schema. Generated from payload. No other schema section exists.

completeness.note: Kernel = 133 nodes (defined + proved). Deferred nodes (6) are not kernel. Scope boundary nodes (3) mark explicit exclusions.

edge_design_notes.theory_building_evaluation: Edges evaluated for theory-building utility. characterizes (equivalence), does_not_imply (separation), strictly_stronger (strict hierarchy), requires_assumption (conditional validity) are high-value for theory building. analogy edges have obstruction fields identifying what blocks formal upgrade. Generalization edges are typed as restricts (constraint removal, conservative) or extends_grammar (new primitives needed, generative).
known_limitations: No explicit trace-type edges (which data presentation enables which success criterion)
analogy obstruction fields are analyst judgments, not formal proofs

nodes.name: Domain
nodes.description: DEFINITION: The input space X. In binary classification, X is typically {0,1}^n, ℝ^d, or a countable set. The choice of X determines what 'instances' are. All other types are parameterized by X.
nodes.formal_definition: The input space X. In binary classification, X is typically {0,1}^n, ℝ^d, or a countable set. The choice of X determines what 'instances' are. All other types are parameterized by X.

provenance.field_origin: Standard mathematical notation. No single introducer.

nodes.name: Label
nodes.description: DEFINITION: The output space Y. For binary classification Y = {0,1} or {-1,+1}. For multiclass Y is finite. For regression Y = ℝ. The choice of Y affects every success criterion and complexity measure downstream.
nodes.formal_definition: The output space Y. For binary classification Y = {0,1} or {-1,+1}. For multiclass Y is finite. For regression Y = ℝ.

provenance.field_origin: Standard mathematical notation. No single introducer.

nodes.name: Alphabet
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Σ = finite set of symbols for formal language theory. A language L ⊆ Σ* is a set of strings over Σ. In Gold's model, the domain is Σ* and concepts are languages. Specialization of Domain for grammatical inference.
nodes.formal_definition: Σ = finite set of symbols for formal language theory. A language L ⊆ Σ* is a set of strings over Σ. In Gold's model, the domain is Σ* and concepts are languages.

provenance.field_origin: Standard formal language theory. Chomsky 1956 onward.

nodes.name: Concept
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A single target function c : X → Y. In binary classification, c : X → {0,1}. The concept is what the learner tries to learn. Also called 'target function' or 'target concept'. Every success criterion is defined relative to a concept.
nodes.formal_definition: A single target function c : X → Y. In binary classification, c : X → {0,1}. The concept is what the learner tries to learn. Also called 'target function' or 'target concept'.

provenance.field_origin: Standard PAC learning terminology. Valiant 1984 formalized.

nodes.name: ConceptClass
nodes.description: DEFINITION: C ⊆ (X → Y) — the set of possible target concepts. Also called 'concept space'. The learner knows C but not which c ∈ C is the target. The complexity of C (measured by VCdim, Ldim, etc.) determines learnability. A concept class is 'learnable' iff it satisfies the characterization conditions. All complexity measures are functions of C.
nodes.formal_definition: C ⊆ (X → Y) — the set of possible target concepts. Also called 'concept space'. The learner knows C but not which c ∈ C is the target.
key_theorems: A concept class is 'learnable' iff it satisfies the characterization conditions

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: HypothesisSpace
nodes.description: DEFINITION: H ⊆ (X → Y) — the set of hypotheses the learner may output. If H = C, learning is 'proper'. If H ⊋ C, learning is 'improper'. FORMAL: def HypothesisSpace (X Y : Type*) := Set (Concept X Y). KEY DISTINCTION: The fundamental theorem holds for C regardless of H choice. But computational complexity can differ dramatically between proper (H = C) and improper (H ⊃ C) learning.
nodes.formal_definition: H ⊆ (X → Y) — the set of hypotheses the learner may output. If H = C, learning is 'proper'. If H ⊋ C, learning is 'improper'.
key_theorems: If H ⊋ C, learning is 'improper'

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

remarks: KEY DISTINCTION: The fundamental theorem holds for C regardless of H choice

nodes.name: Proper
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Learning is 'proper' if the output h must belong to C (the concept class), 'improper' if h may come from a larger H ⊋ C. FORMAL: Proper ↔ H ⊆ C. KEY RESULT: Pitt-Valiant 1988: 3-term DNF is NP-hard proper, poly-time improper. No sample complexity separation in standard PAC, but separation exists in DP-PAC. TYPE IMPACT: The Learner's output type must be parameterized — sometimes H = C, sometimes H ⊃ C.
nodes.formal_definition: Learning is 'proper' if the output h must belong to C (the concept class), 'improper' if h may come from a larger H ⊋ C.

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

remarks: DEFINITION: Learning is 'proper' if the output h must belong to C (the concept class), 'improper' if h may come from a larger H ⊋ C

nodes.name: Hypothesis
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A single element h ∈ H — the learner's current guess of the target concept. In Gold's model, the learner outputs a sequence of hypotheses h₁, h₂, ... that must converge. In PAC, the learner outputs one h after seeing the sample. QUALITY: Measured by generalization error R(h) = P_{x∼D}[h(x) ≠ c(x)].
nodes.formal_definition: A single element h ∈ H — the learner's current guess of the target concept. In Gold's model, the learner outputs a sequence of hypotheses h₁, h₂, ... that must converge. In PAC, the learner outputs one h after seeing the sample. QUALITY: Measured by generalization error R(h) = P_{x∼D}[h(x) ≠ c(x)].

provenance.field_origin: Standard statistical learning terminology.

nodes.name: TargetConcept
nodes.description: DEFINITION: The actual concept c* ∈ C that generated the data. The learner doesn't know c* but sees data consistent with it. In realizability: c* ∈ H. In agnostic: c* may not be in H. FORMAL: variable (target : Concept X Y). ROLE: The ground truth against which all hypotheses are evaluated.
nodes.formal_definition: The actual concept c* ∈ C that generated the data. The learner doesn't know c* but sees data consistent with it. In realizability: c* ∈ H. In agnostic: c* may not be in H.

provenance.field_origin: Standard PAC learning terminology.

nodes.name: InductiveBias
nodes.description: DEFINITION: The set of assumptions a learner uses to generalize beyond training data. Formally encoded as the choice of H and the learning algorithm. THEOREM (NFL): Without inductive bias, no learner beats random guessing on average over all targets. EXAMPLES: Choosing H = linear functions is a bias toward simplicity. Regularization is a bias toward smooth functions. The prior in PAC-Bayes is an explicit inductive bias. ROLE: NFL says this is MANDATORY for learning. Every learner has one, explicitly or implicitly.
nodes.formal_definition: The set of assumptions a learner uses to generalize beyond training data. Formally encoded as the choice of H and the learning algorithm.
key_theorems: THEOREM (NFL): Without inductive bias, no learner beats random guessing on average over all targets

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: VersionSpace
nodes.description: DEFINITION: VS(H, data) = {h ∈ H : h consistent with all observed (x,y) pairs}. PROPERTIES: Starts as all of H (no data). Shrinks monotonically as data arrives. Learning succeeds when VS shrinks to a single hypothesis (or a set with low error). MITCHELL'S ALGORITHM: Candidate elimination maintains most-general and most-specific boundaries. VERSION SPACE SIZE: Related to teaching dimension (min examples to shrink VS to 1 concept).
nodes.formal_definition: VS(H, data) = {h ∈ H : h consistent with all observed (x,y) pairs}.
key_theorems: MITCHELL'S ALGORITHM: Candidate elimination maintains most-general and most-specific boundaries

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: TimeIndex
nodes.description: DEFINITION: t ∈ ℕ (or Ordinal for transfinite settings) — the time parameter for sequential/non-stationary learning. In concept drift: target c_t varies with t. In online learning: adversary presents examples sequentially. In mind-change hierarchy: ordinal time indexes revision steps.
nodes.formal_definition: t ∈ ℕ (or Ordinal for transfinite settings) — the time parameter for sequential/non-stationary learning.

provenance.field_origin: Standard mathematical notation for sequential processes.

nodes.name: DataStream
nodes.description: DEFINITION: σ : ℕ → X × Y — an infinite labeled sequence. The learner sees σ(1), σ(2), ... sequentially. VARIANTS: Text presentation (all labels positive, σ(i) ∈ L). Informant presentation (labels indicate membership, σ(i) = (x, x∈L?)). ROLE: The data presentation type for Gold-style identification in the limit.
nodes.formal_definition: σ : ℕ → X × Y — an infinite labeled sequence. The learner sees σ(1), σ(2), ... sequentially.

provenance.field_origin: Standard Gold-model terminology. Gold 1967.

nodes.name: TextPresentation
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A data stream where every example is a positive member of the target language L. Formally: σ : ℕ → L such that range(σ) = L (every element of L eventually appears). GOLD'S THEOREM: Superfinite classes are NOT identifiable from text alone. This is the strongest negative result about passive learning: positive examples are insufficient for certain classes. ROLE: The weakest data presentation — no negative information.
nodes.formal_definition: A data stream where every example is a positive member of the target language L. Formally: σ : ℕ → L such that range(σ) = L (every element of L eventually appears).
key_theorems: GOLD'S THEOREM: Superfinite classes are NOT identifiable from text alone

provenance.field_origin: Standard data model in learning theory. See references in description.

nodes.name: InformantPresentation
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A data stream presenting both positive and negative examples. Formally: σ : ℕ → X × {0,1} such that every x ∈ X eventually appears with its correct label. CONTRAST WITH TEXT: From informant, every countable class over countable domain is identifiable in the limit. The negative examples provide crucial information that text lacks.
nodes.formal_definition: A data stream presenting both positive and negative examples. Formally: σ : ℕ → X × {0,1} such that every x ∈ X eventually appears with its correct label.

provenance.field_origin: Standard data model in learning theory. See references in description.

nodes.name: IIDSample
nodes.description: DEFINITION: S = ((x₁,y₁),...,(x_m,y_m)) where each (x_i,y_i) is drawn independently from distribution D over X×Y. ROLE: The data model for PAC learning. Key assumption: training and test distributions are the same D. SAMPLE COMPLEXITY: m(ε,δ) = number of i.i.d. samples needed for P[error ≤ ε] ≥ 1-δ. For VCdim=d: m = Θ(d/ε + log(1/δ)/ε) optimal (Hanneke 2016).
nodes.formal_definition: S = ((x₁,y₁),...,(x_m,y_m)) where each (x_i,y_i) is drawn independently from distribution D over X×Y.

provenance.field_origin: Standard data model in learning theory. See references in description.

nodes.name: MembershipOracle
nodes.description: DEFINITION: MQ(x) returns 1 if x ∈ target, 0 otherwise. The learner actively queries specific points. POWER: MQ + EQ together enable Angluin's L* algorithm for exact DFA learning. MQ alone: some classes need exponentially many queries that EQ resolves in poly. QUERY COMPLEXITY: Number of MQ calls needed.
nodes.formal_definition: MQ(x) returns 1 if x ∈ target, 0 otherwise. The learner actively queries specific points.

provenance.field_origin: Standard data model in learning theory. See references in description.

nodes.name: EquivalenceOracle
nodes.description: DEFINITION: EQ(h) returns 'YES' if h = target, or a counterexample x where h(x) ≠ target(x) otherwise. This is a CRISP oracle — ground truth, not approximate. The verification primitive in exact learning and CEGIS. HALVING ALGORITHM: With EQ alone, ⌈log₂|H|⌉ queries suffice (binary search on version space).
nodes.formal_definition: EQ(h) returns 'YES' if h = target, or a counterexample x where h(x) ≠ target(x) otherwise. This is a CRISP oracle — ground truth, not approximate.

provenance.field_origin: Standard data model in learning theory. See references in description.

nodes.name: Counterexample
nodes.description: DEFINITION: An input x where hypothesis h disagrees with target c: h(x) ≠ c(x). ROLE: The feedback from an equivalence oracle. Each counterexample eliminates at least one hypothesis from the version space. In CEGIS, counterexamples drive the refinement loop. In Littlestone's model, the adversary chooses instances to force mistakes = counterexamples.
nodes.formal_definition: An input x where hypothesis h disagrees with target c: h(x) ≠ c(x).

provenance.field_origin: Standard query learning terminology. Angluin 1987.

remarks: ROLE: The feedback from an equivalence oracle

nodes.name: Advice
nodes.description: DEFINITION: α — auxiliary information provided to the learner beyond the standard data stream. Four oracle types (Kaufmann-Stephan 2001): LIST (enumerates class members), PREDICTOR (forecasts next values), CLASSIFIER (accepts class members), MARTINGALE (gains on class members). Lists most powerful for Ex-learning. KEY RESULT: Robust learning (succeeds with EVERY oracle of given Turing degree) = unaided learning. The TYPE of advice matters more than its computational power.
nodes.formal_definition: α — auxiliary information provided to the learner beyond the standard data stream. Four oracle types (Kaufmann-Stephan 2001): LIST (enumerates class members), PREDICTOR (forecasts next values), CLASSIFIER (accepts class members), MARTINGALE (gains on class members). Lists most powerful for Ex-learning.

provenance.field_origin: Standard data model in learning theory. See references in description.

nodes.name: NoisyInput
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Data where labels or features may be corrupted. NOISE MODELS: Random classification noise: each label flipped independently with probability η < 1/2. Malicious noise (Valiant 1985): fraction η of examples replaced by adversarial (x,y) pairs. Nasty noise (Bshouty et al. 2002): adversary sees the correct example before choosing corruption. LEARNABILITY: Finite VCdim classes are learnable under random noise. Under malicious noise, error ≥ η/(1-η) is unavoidable.
nodes.formal_definition: Data where labels or features may be corrupted.

provenance.field_origin: Angluin-Laird 1988 formalized noise models for PAC.

nodes.name: Learner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: An algorithm that maps data to hypotheses. FORMAL: class Learner (X Y H : Type*) where State : Type*; init : State; update : State → (X × Y) → State; output : State → H. The State type is associated — for history-based learners State = List(X×Y), for iterative learners State = H, for Bayesian learners State = Distribution(H). ROLE: The central agent type. All success criteria are predicates on Learner behavior.
nodes.formal_definition: An algorithm that maps data to hypotheses.

provenance.field_origin: Abstract agent type. Formalized differently in each paradigm.

nodes.name: IterativeLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner whose hypothesis update depends only on current hypothesis and latest datum, not full history. Formally: State = H, update(h, (x,y)) = h'. POWER: Strictly weaker than general learners for some classes. EXAMPLE: Perceptron algorithm is iterative. Gradient descent is iterative. ADVANTAGE: O(1) memory per step (doesn't store data).
nodes.formal_definition: A learner whose hypothesis update depends only on current hypothesis and latest datum, not full history. Formally: State = H, update(h, (x,y)) = h'.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: SetDrivenLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner whose output depends only on the SET of examples seen, not their ORDER. Formally: output is invariant under permutation of data sequence. POWER: Strictly weaker than order-dependent learners. Most 'natural' learners (ERM, Bayesian update) are set-driven. SIGNIFICANCE: If a class is learnable by a set-driven learner, it's learnable 'without memory of order'.
nodes.formal_definition: A learner whose output depends only on the SET of examples seen, not their ORDER. Formally: output is invariant under permutation of data sequence.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: ConsistentLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner that always outputs a hypothesis consistent with ALL observed data. Formally: ∀ data prefix, output(data) agrees with every (x,y) in data. POWER: Consistent learners can learn anything learnable in Ex-identification. KEY PROPERTY: Consistency + Occam's razor → PAC learning (short consistent hypotheses generalize).
nodes.formal_definition: A learner that always outputs a hypothesis consistent with ALL observed data. Formally: ∀ data prefix, output(data) agrees with every (x,y) in data.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: ConservativeLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner that only changes its hypothesis when forced by contradicting data. Formally: if new datum is consistent with current hypothesis, hypothesis unchanged. MIND CHANGES: Conservative learners minimize mind changes (at most one per contradiction). TRADE-OFF: Slow to adapt but stable. Related to conservative online algorithms.
nodes.formal_definition: A learner that only changes its hypothesis when forced by contradicting data. Formally: if new datum is consistent with current hypothesis, hypothesis unchanged.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: ActiveLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner that selects which data points to query rather than passively receiving data. FORMALLY: interleaves queries (asking for label of chosen x) with hypothesis updates. LABEL COMPLEXITY: Number of labels requested. Can be much less than sample complexity. KEY RESULT: For some classes, active learning is exponentially more label-efficient than passive (e.g., thresholds on [0,1]: O(log(1/ε)) active vs O(1/ε) passive). UNIVERSAL RATES: Hanneke 2024 — tetrachotomy for active learning.
nodes.formal_definition: A learner that selects which data points to query rather than passively receiving data.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: PassiveLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner with no control over training examples — learns from whatever is provided. MODELS: PAC (i.i.d. from D), Gold (text/informant sequence), online (adversarially chosen). Most classical analysis assumes passive learning. CONTRAST: Active learners can be exponentially more efficient.
nodes.formal_definition: A learner with no control over training examples — learns from whatever is provided. MODELS: PAC (i.i.d. from D), Gold (text/informant sequence), online (adversarially chosen). Most classical analysis assumes passive learning.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: ProbabilisticLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner that uses internal randomness. May randomize hypothesis updates, queries, or other decisions. POWER: Can sometimes learn classes deterministic learners cannot (with same resources). In team learning: a probabilistic learner simulates a team. ERM with random tie-breaking is probabilistic.
nodes.formal_definition: A learner that uses internal randomness. May randomize hypothesis updates, queries, or other decisions.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: TeamLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: [m,n]-learning: a team of n learners, succeed if at least m succeed. Formally: Fin n → Learner, success = at least m members converge to correct hypothesis. HIERARCHY (Smith 1982): [1,n+1]-Ex ⊋ [1,n]-Ex. Teams strictly increase learning power. CONNECTION: Probabilistic learning with success probability > m/n simulates [m,n]-team learning.
nodes.formal_definition: [m,n]-learning: a team of n learners, succeed if at least m succeed. Formally: Fin n → Learner, success = at least m members converge to correct hypothesis.
key_theorems: HIERARCHY (Smith 1982): [1,n+1]-Ex ⊋ [1,n]-Ex
connections: CONNECTION: Probabilistic learning with success probability > m/n simulates [m,n]-team learning

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: LearnerWithAdvice
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner augmented with an auxiliary information channel α. FORMALLY: LearnerWithAdvice α = (advice : α) → Learner. KAUFMANN-STEPHAN 2001: Four oracle types with different powers. Lists > predictors ≈ classifiers > martingales for Ex-learning. ROBUST LEARNING: Succeeding with EVERY oracle of given degree = unaided learning. Advice type matters more than computational power.
nodes.formal_definition: A learner augmented with an auxiliary information channel α.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: MetaLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner that learns which learning setup to deploy. Formally: given tasks P₁,...,P_n drawn from environment Q, selects hypothesis space H_λ from family {H_λ : λ ∈ Λ}. BAXTER 2000: Total error ≈ O(complexity(H_λ)/m + complexity(Λ)/n). When n=1, |Λ|=1: reduces to standard PAC. Per-task sample complexity decreases as n (number of tasks) grows. ROLE: Formalizes 'learning to learn' / AutoML / inductive bias selection.
nodes.formal_definition: A learner that learns which learning setup to deploy. Formally: given tasks P₁,...,P_n drawn from environment Q, selects hypothesis space H_λ from family {H_λ : λ ∈ Λ}. BAXTER 2000: Total error ≈ O(complexity(H_λ)/m + complexity(Λ)/n). When n=1, |Λ|=1: reduces to standard PAC. Per-task sample complexity decreases as n (number of tasks) grows.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: Teacher
nodes.description: DEFINITION: An entity that provides information to the learner. Typeclass with instances: optimal (min teaching set), adversarial (worst case), random (stochastic), minimally adequate (MQ+EQ). TEACHING DIMENSION: Min examples an optimal teacher needs to identify target. ROLE: The 'other player' in the learning game.
nodes.formal_definition: An entity that provides information to the learner. Typeclass with instances: optimal (min teaching set), adversarial (worst case), random (stochastic), minimally adequate (MQ+EQ).

provenance.field_origin: Abstract agent type. Goldman-Kearns 1995 formalized teaching dimension.

nodes.name: OptimalTeacher
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Selects minimum-size teaching set T such that T uniquely identifies target c in C. |T| = teaching dimension of c in C. GOLDMAN-KEARNS 1995: Teaching dimension can be much larger than VC dimension. ZHU 2015: Machine teaching as inverse problem to machine learning.
nodes.formal_definition: Selects minimum-size teaching set T such that T uniquely identifies target c in C. |T| = teaching dimension of c in C.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: AdversarialTeacher
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Chooses data sequence to maximize learner difficulty (worst case). In online learning: adversary picks (x_t, y_t) after seeing learner's prediction. POWER: Littlestone dimension characterizes worst-case mistake count against adversary. ROLE: The 'nature' in minimax analysis of online learning.
nodes.formal_definition: Chooses data sequence to maximize learner difficulty (worst case). In online learning: adversary picks (x_t, y_t) after seeing learner's prediction.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: RandomTeacher
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Provides data with some randomness — not adversarial, not optimal. In PAC: D is a random teacher (i.i.d. from unknown distribution). In noise models: random label flipping.
nodes.formal_definition: Provides data with some randomness — not adversarial, not optimal. In PAC: D is a random teacher (i.i.d. from unknown distribution). In noise models: random label flipping.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: MinimallyAdequateTeacher
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Angluin 1987): Answers both membership queries and equivalence queries. 'Minimally adequate' = exactly enough for exact learning. L* ALGORITHM: Uses MQ+EQ to exactly learn unknown DFA in time polynomial in |DFA| and length of longest counterexample. ROLE: The canonical active learning teacher.
nodes.formal_definition: (Angluin 1987): Answers both membership queries and equivalence queries. 'Minimally adequate' = exactly enough for exact learning. L* ALGORITHM: Uses MQ+EQ to exactly learn unknown DFA in time polynomial in |DFA| and length of longest counterexample.
key_theorems: DEFINITION (Angluin 1987): Answers both membership queries and equivalence queries
open_problems: L* ALGORITHM: Uses MQ+EQ to exactly learn unknown DFA in time polynomial in |DFA| and length of longest counterexample

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: LLMCritic
nodes.description: DEFINITION: An LLM used as approximate verifier/oracle. CEGIS applicability: CEGIS requires crisp oracle. LLM critic breaks CEGIS convergence guarantees. Use reward-guided search instead.
nodes.formal_definition: An LLM used as approximate verifier/oracle. CEGIS applicability: CEGIS requires crisp oracle. LLM critic breaks CEGIS convergence guarantees. Use reward-guided search instead.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.name: Synthesizer
nodes.description: DEFINITION: The component that generates candidate programs from specification. In CEGIS: proposes solutions checked by verifier. APPROACHES: Enumerative (brute-force), constraint-based (SMT solver), neural (LLM-guided). ROLE: The hypothesis generator in program synthesis
nodes.formal_definition: The component that generates candidate programs from specification. In CEGIS: proposes solutions checked by verifier. APPROACHES: Enumerative (brute-force), constraint-based (SMT solver), neural (LLM-guided).
connections: Produces candidates that the verifier checks

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.scope_note: Phase 2 (program synthesis layer). Not in formalization kernel.

nodes.name: Verifier
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Checks whether candidate satisfies specification. Returns YES or counterexample. In CEGIS: the crisp oracle. DISTINCTION: Crisp verifier (formal methods, compiler) enables CEGIS convergence. Fuzzy verifier (LLM, reward model) does not.
nodes.formal_definition: Checks whether candidate satisfies specification. Returns YES or counterexample. In CEGIS: the crisp oracle. Crisp verifier (formal methods, compiler) enables CEGIS convergence. Fuzzy verifier (LLM, reward model) does not.

provenance.field_origin: Specialization of Learner typeclass. See references in description.

nodes.scope_note: Phase 2 (program synthesis layer). Not in formalization kernel.

nodes.name: Realizable
nodes.description: DEFINITION: The assumption that target c* ∈ C (or c* ∈ H). Formally: ∃ h ∈ H with R(h) = 0. IMPACT ON SAMPLE COMPLEXITY: Realizable PAC needs Θ(d/ε) samples. Agnostic needs Θ(d/ε²). The quadratic blowup is the 'price of agnosticism'. ROLE: A type-level flag on success criteria. Must be explicit parameter, not silent assumption.
nodes.formal_definition: The assumption that target c* ∈ H, i.e., ∃ h ∈ H with R(h) = 0. In PAC: given as explicit parameter on success criterion. Realizable PAC sample complexity: Θ(d/ε + log(1/δ)/ε). Agnostic (no realizability): Θ(d/ε² + log(1/δ)/ε²). The quadratic blowup is the price of agnosticism.

provenance.field_origin: Standard PAC assumption. Implicit in Valiant 1984, named later.

nodes.name: Agnostic
nodes.description: DEFINITION: No assumption that target ∈ H. Success = compete with best in H: R(h) ≤ inf_{h'∈H} R(h') + ε. HAUSSLER 1992: Introduced agnostic PAC. Sample complexity Θ(d/ε²). VC dim characterizes both realizable and agnostic learnability for binary classification. ROLE: The realistic setting — models are always wrong, question is how wrong.
nodes.formal_definition: No assumption that target ∈ H. Success criterion: output h with R(h) ≤ inf_{h'∈H} R(h') + ε, with probability ≥ 1-δ. Sample complexity: Θ(d/ε² + log(1/δ)/ε²) where d = VCdim(H). Introduced by Haussler 1992. The realistic setting: models are always wrong, question is how wrong.

provenance.field_origin: Named by Haussler 1992. The concept of model misspecification predates.

nodes.name: ExLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Gold 1967): Identification in the limit. Learner receives data σ(1), σ(2),... and outputs hypothesis sequence h₁, h₂,.... Success: ∃ t₀ s.t. ∀ t ≥ t₀, h_t = h* where h* = correct hypothesis index. FORMALLY: The hypothesis sequence syntactically converges to a single correct program. HIERARCHY: FIN ⊂ Ex₀ ⊂ Ex₁ ⊂ ... ⊂ Ex* ⊂ BC₀ ⊂ ... ⊂ BC*. All inclusions strict.
nodes.formal_definition: Identification in the limit (Gold 1967). Learner receives data σ(1), σ(2),... and outputs hypothesis sequence h₁, h₂,.... Success: ∃ t₀ s.t. ∀ t ≥ t₀, h_t = h* where h* is a correct hypothesis index (φ_{h*} = target). The hypothesis sequence must syntactically converge to a single correct program index.

nodes.name: BCLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Behaviorally correct learning. The learner's outputs eventually agree with target on ALL inputs, but the hypothesis INDEX may keep changing. Formally: ∃ t₀ s.t. ∀ t ≥ t₀, φ_{h_t} = c (extensional equality, not syntactic). POWER: Strictly more powerful than Ex. BC can learn some classes Ex cannot (hypothesis may keep being revised as long as each revision computes the same function).
nodes.formal_definition: Behaviorally correct learning (Case-Smith 1983). The learner's outputs eventually agree with target on ALL inputs, but the hypothesis INDEX may keep changing. Formally: ∃ t₀ s.t. ∀ t ≥ t₀, φ_{h_t} = c (extensional equality, not syntactic). Strictly more powerful than Ex: BC can learn classes Ex cannot.
key_theorems: POWER: Strictly more powerful than Ex

nodes.name: FiniteLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Learner must identify target after finite data and announce completion. Formally: learner outputs at most one non-trivial hypothesis, which must be correct. POWER: Strictly weaker than Ex (must commit without possibility of revision). TEACHING DIMENSION: The minimum number of examples for finite identification = teaching dimension.
nodes.formal_definition: Learner must identify target after finite data and announce completion. Formally: learner outputs at most one non-trivial hypothesis, which must be correct.

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: VacillatoryLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION: TxtFex_b — learner converges to a set of at most b correct hypotheses, may alternate among them. HIERARCHY (Case 1999): TxtFex₁ = TxtEx ⊂ TxtFex₂ ⊂ ... ⊂ TxtFex* ⊂ TxtBc (all strict for language learning). For function learning: vacillatory collapses to Ex (Barzdin-Podnieks 1973).
nodes.formal_definition: TxtFex_b — learner converges to a set of at most b correct hypotheses, may alternate among them.

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: AnomalousLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Ex_a — learner converges to hypothesis correct except on at most a inputs. Ex* allows finitely many anomalies without fixed bound. HIERARCHY: Ex₀ = Ex ⊂ Ex₁ ⊂ Ex₂ ⊂ ... ⊂ Ex* ⊂ BC₀. Each step strictly increases learning power.
nodes.formal_definition: Ex_a — learner converges to hypothesis correct except on at most a inputs. Ex* allows finitely many anomalies without fixed bound.
key_theorems: Ex* allows finitely many anomalies without fixed bound

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: PACLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Valiant 1984): C is PAC learnable if ∃ algorithm A s.t. ∀ c ∈ C, ∀ D over X, ∀ ε,δ > 0: given m ≥ m(ε,δ) i.i.d. samples from D labeled by c, A outputs h with P[R_D(h) ≤ ε] ≥ 1-δ, running in time poly(1/ε, 1/δ, n, size(c)). FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM: PAC learnable ↔ finite VCdim ↔ 7 other equivalent conditions. SAMPLE COMPLEXITY: Θ(d/ε + log(1/δ)/ε) optimal. Θ(d·log(1/ε)/ε) for ERM.
nodes.formal_definition: Probably Approximately Correct learning (Valiant 1984). C is PAC learnable if ∃ algorithm A s.t. ∀ c ∈ C, ∀ distribution D over X, ∀ ε,δ > 0: given m ≥ m(ε,δ) i.i.d. samples from D labeled by c, A outputs h with P[R_D(h) ≤ ε] ≥ 1-δ. Fundamental theorem: PAC learnable ↔ VCdim(C) < ∞. Optimal sample complexity: Θ(d/ε + log(1/δ)/ε) (Hanneke 2016).
key_theorems: FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM: PAC learnable ↔ finite VCdim ↔ 7 other equivalent conditions

nodes.name: AgnosticPACLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Haussler 1992): No realizability. Success: P[R(h) ≤ inf_{h'∈H} R(h') + ε] ≥ 1-δ. SAMPLE COMPLEXITY: Θ(d/ε² + log(1/δ)/ε²) — quadratic in 1/ε vs linear for realizable. CHARACTERIZATION: Finite VCdim ↔ agnostic PAC learnable (same condition as realizable, but sample complexity is worse).
nodes.formal_definition: (Haussler 1992): No realizability. Success: P[R(h) ≤ inf_{h'∈H} R(h') + ε] ≥ 1-δ.
key_theorems: CHARACTERIZATION: Finite VCdim ↔ agnostic PAC learnable (same condition as realizable, but sample complexity is worse)

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: ExactLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Learner must identify target EXACTLY with zero error. Typically via queries (MQ+EQ). ANGLUIN 1987: DFAs are exactly learnable with MQ+EQ in poly time (L* algorithm). QUERY COMPLEXITY: Number of queries to identify target. For DFA with n states: O(n²|Σ|) MQs and n EQs.
nodes.formal_definition: Learner must identify target EXACTLY with zero error. Typically via queries (MQ+EQ).

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: MistakeBounded
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Littlestone 1988): In online learning, total prediction errors ≤ M regardless of adversary's sequence. Formally: ∀ adversarial sequences of (x_t, y_t), Σ 𝟙[ĥ_t(x_t) ≠ y_t] ≤ M. CHARACTERIZATION: Optimal M = Ldim(H). Achieved by SOA (Standard Optimal Algorithm) which maintains version space and halves it. HALVING: Mistakes ≤ log₂|H|. SOA: Mistakes ≤ Ldim(H) ≤ log₂|H|.
nodes.formal_definition: Online learning with total prediction errors bounded by M regardless of adversary sequence. Formally: for all adversarial sequences (x₁,y₁),(x₂,y₂),..., the total mistakes Σ 𝟙[ŷ_t ≠ y_t] ≤ M. Optimal M = Ldim(H), achieved by SOA (Standard Optimal Algorithm). Halving algorithm achieves log₂|H| mistakes.
key_theorems: CHARACTERIZATION: Optimal M = Ldim(H)

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: RegretBounded
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Regret_T = Σ_{t=1}^T ℓ(ĥ_t, z_t) - min_{h∈H} Σ_{t=1}^T ℓ(h, z_t). Success: Regret_T/T → 0. ACHIEVABLE RATES: For finite |H|: O(√(T·log|H|)) via Hedge/exponential weights. For infinite H with Ldim=d: O(√(d·T·log T)). ROLE: The online learning analog of generalization error. Sublinear regret = asymptotically as good as best fixed hypothesis.
nodes.formal_definition: Regret_T = Σ_{t=1}^T ℓ(ĥ_t, z_t) - min_{h∈H} Σ_{t=1}^T ℓ(h, z_t). Success: Regret_T/T → 0.

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: OnlineLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Sequential prediction with feedback. Protocol: (1) adversary chooses (x_t, y_t), (2) learner sees x_t and predicts ŷ_t, (3) learner sees y_t and updates. Mistake-bounded characterization. Regret-bounded characterization. CONTRAST WITH PAC: PAC = batch (all data at once, i.i.d.). Online = sequential (one at a time, adversarial).
nodes.formal_definition: Sequential prediction with adversarial feedback. Protocol per round t: (1) adversary chooses (x_t, y_t), (2) learner sees x_t, predicts ŷ_t, (3) learner sees y_t, updates. Realizable setting: Ldim characterizes optimal mistake bound. Agnostic: regret Θ(√(Ldim·T)) characterizes performance. Fundamentally different from PAC: sequential not batch, adversarial not stochastic.
key_theorems: Mistake-bounded characterization
Regret-bounded characterization

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: MonotonicLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Hypothesis sequence is monotonic w.r.t. set inclusion. STRONG: Each hypothesis generalizes the previous (never loses correct instances). WEAK: Each hypothesis specializes the previous (never gains incorrect instances)
nodes.formal_definition: Hypothesis sequence is monotonic w.r.t. set inclusion. STRONG: Each hypothesis generalizes the previous (never loses correct instances). WEAK: Each hypothesis specializes the previous (never gains incorrect instances).

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: TrialAndErrorLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Learning by proposing hypotheses and revising upon error. Equivalent to identification in the limit (Ex-learning). Each error triggers a 'mind change'. LIMITING RECURSION (Gold 1965, Putnam 1965): A function is limit computable iff computable by trial-and-error. The Δ⁰₂ sets are exactly those decidable by trial-and-error.
nodes.formal_definition: Learning by proposing hypotheses and revising upon error. Equivalent to identification in the limit (Ex-learning). Each error triggers a 'mind change'. LIMITING RECURSION (Gold 1965, Putnam 1965): A function is limit computable iff computable by trial-and-error. The Δ⁰₂ sets are exactly those decidable by trial-and-error.
key_theorems: Equivalent to identification in the limit (Ex-learning)
LIMITING RECURSION (Gold 1965, Putnam 1965): A function is limit computable iff computable by trial-and-error

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: ExUnderDrift
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Ex-learning with time-varying target c_t. Standard Ex convergence is impossible (target moves). Replacement criteria: frequency learning (fraction correct in sliding window), density learning (limit inferior of correct fraction ≥ q), martingale learning (unbounded accumulated wealth). HELMBOLD-LONG 1994: Drift rate Δ = P[c_t ≠ c_{t+1}]. Tracking error O((d·Δ)^{1/3}). NOT a parameterization of Ex — the success criterion changes fundamentally.
nodes.formal_definition: Ex-learning with time-varying target c_t. Standard Ex convergence is impossible (target moves). Replacement criteria: frequency learning (fraction correct in sliding window), density learning (limit inferior of correct fraction ≥ q), martingale learning (unbounded accumulated wealth).
key_theorems: Replacement criteria: frequency learning (fraction correct in sliding window), density learning (limit inferior of correct fraction ≥ q), martingale learning (unbounded accumulated wealth)

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: UniversalLearning
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Bousquet et al. 2021): H is learnable at rate R if ∃ algorithm s.t. ∀ realizable D, constants C,c exist with E[R(ĥ_n)] ≤ C·R(c·n). Constants may depend on D. TRICHOTOMY: For |H|≥3, exactly one of: (1) No infinite Littlestone tree → rate e^{−n}. (2) Infinite Littlestone tree, no infinite VCL tree → rate 1/n. (3) Infinite VCL tree → arbitrarily slow. GENERALIZES PAC: PAC = universal + uniformity over distributions. PAC learnable ⊊ universally learnable.
nodes.formal_definition: A class H is universally learnable at rate R(n) if ∃ algorithm s.t. for all realizable distributions D, ∃ constants C,c (depending on D) with E[R(ĥ_n)] ≤ C·R(c·n). The trichotomy (Bousquet et al. 2021): for |H|≥3, exactly one of: (1) no infinite Littlestone tree → rate e^{-n}, (2) infinite Littlestone tree, no infinite VCL tree → rate 1/n, (3) infinite VCL tree → arbitrarily slow rates.
key_theorems: PAC learnable ⊊ universally learnable

nodes.name: VCDimension
nodes.description: DEFINITION: VCdim(H) = largest d s.t. ∃ {x₁,...,x_d} ⊂ X shattered by H. Shattering: ∀ labeling (y₁,...,y_d) ∈ {0,1}^d, ∃ h ∈ H with h(x_i) = y_i ∀i. EXAMPLES: Halfplanes in ℝ²: VCdim=3. Intervals on ℝ: VCdim=2. Thresholds on ℝ: VCdim=1. All functions {0,1}^n → {0,1}: VCdim=2^n. FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM: Finite VCdim ↔ PAC learnable ↔ 7 other conditions. SAMPLE COMPLEXITY: PAC with VCdim=d needs Θ(d/ε) samples (optimal).
nodes.formal_definition: VCdim(H) = largest d s.t. ∃ {x₁,...,x_d} ⊂ X shattered by H. Shattering: ∀ labeling (y₁,...,y_d) ∈ {0,1}^d, ∃ h ∈ H with h(x_i) = y_i ∀i.
key_theorems: FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM: Finite VCdim ↔ PAC learnable ↔ 7 other conditions

nodes.name: Shatters
nodes.description: DEFINITION: H shatters set S = {x₁,...,x_d} if for every labeling (y₁,...,y_d) ∈ {0,1}^d, ∃ h ∈ H with h(x_i) = y_i ∀i. Equivalently: |{h|_S : h ∈ H}| = 2^|S|. ROLE: The primitive predicate underlying VCdim. VCdim(H) = max{|S| : H shatters S}.
nodes.formal_definition: H shatters set S = {x₁,...,x_d} if for every labeling (y₁,...,y_d) ∈ {0,1}^d, ∃ h ∈ H with h(x_i) = y_i ∀i. Equivalently: |{h|_S : h ∈ H}| = 2^|S|.
key_theorems: Equivalently: |{h|_S : h ∈ H}| = 2^|S|

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: LittlestoneDimension
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Ldim(H) = max depth of complete binary tree shattered by H. A tree is shattered if: for every root-to-leaf path (ε₁,...,ε_d) ∈ {0,1}^d, ∃ h ∈ H with h(x_t) = ε_t ∀t, where x_t is the node at level t along the path. KEY DISTINCTION: VC shatters SETS (non-adaptive). Littlestone shatters TREES (adaptive). Always VCdim ≤ Ldim. Gap can be infinite. CHARACTERIZATION: Ldim < ∞ ↔ online learnable with finite mistakes ↔ DP-PAC learnable.
nodes.formal_definition: Ldim(H) = max depth of complete binary tree shattered by H. A tree is shattered if: for every root-to-leaf path (ε₁,...,ε_d) ∈ {0,1}^d, ∃ h ∈ H with h(x_t) = ε_t ∀t, where x_t is the node at level t along the path.
key_theorems: CHARACTERIZATION: Ldim < ∞ ↔ online learnable with finite mistakes ↔ DP-PAC learnable

nodes.name: MistakeTree
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A complete binary tree labeled by instances from X. Each internal node has instance x, left child = 'predict 0' branch, right child = 'predict 1' branch. Used to define Littlestone dimension: Ldim(H) = max depth of mistake tree shattered by H.
nodes.formal_definition: A complete binary tree labeled by instances from X. Each internal node has instance x, left child = 'predict 0' branch, right child = 'predict 1' branch. Used to define Littlestone dimension: Ldim(H) = max depth of mistake tree shattered by H.

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: OrdinalLittlestoneDim
nodes.description: DEFINITION: When Littlestone tree is well-founded (no infinite branch), its Cantor-Bendixson rank is a countable ordinal. Recursively: ρ(leaf)=0, ρ(node)=sup{ρ(child)+1}. ROLE: Controls constants in universal learning exponential regime. Directly parallels Freivalds-Smith ordinal mind-change hierarchy. When Ldim=d<∞, ordinal version = d. When Ldim=∞ but no infinite tree: ordinal ≥ ω.
nodes.formal_definition: When Littlestone tree is well-founded (no infinite branch), its Cantor-Bendixson rank is a countable ordinal. Recursively: ρ(leaf)=0, ρ(node)=sup{ρ(child)+1}.
connections: Directly parallels Freivalds-Smith ordinal mind-change hierarchy

nodes.name: VCLTree
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Bousquet et al. 2021): Complete tree where node at depth k carries k+1 points from X, branching factor 2^{k+1}. At each level, the 2^{k+1} branches enumerate all labelings of the k+1 points — VC shattering at each node. ROLE: Defines second threshold in universal learning trichotomy. Infinite VCL tree → arbitrarily slow universal rates. A VCL tree is a Littlestone tree with VC shattering constraints at growing nodes.
nodes.formal_definition: (Bousquet et al. 2021): Complete tree where node at depth k carries k+1 points from X, branching factor 2^{k+1}. At each level, the 2^{k+1} branches enumerate all labelings of the k+1 points — VC shattering at each node.

nodes.name: StarNumber
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Hanneke-Yang 2015): For h ∈ H, s_h(H) = largest n s.t. ∃ x₁,...,x_n where ∀i ∈ {0,...,n}, ∃ h_i ∈ H agreeing with h on all x_j except j=i. Star number s̄(H) = sup_h s_h(H). THEOREM (Hanneke 2024): s̄(H) = VC(V(H)) where V(H) is collection of version spaces. FUNDAMENTAL INCOMPATIBILITY: No infinite class has both finite Ldim and finite star number. KEY: star·Ldim ≥ log|H| for finite H.
nodes.formal_definition: (Hanneke-Yang 2015): For h ∈ H, s_h(H) = largest n s.t. ∃ x₁,...,x_n where ∀i ∈ {0,...,n}, ∃ h_i ∈ H agreeing with h on all x_j except j=i. Star number s̄(H) = sup_h s_h(H).
key_theorems: THEOREM (Hanneke 2024): s̄(H) = VC(V(H)) where V(H) is collection of version spaces

nodes.name: EluderDimension
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Threshold dimension of version spaces. Controls active learning query complexity. Introduced in Russo-Van Roy 2013 for RL, connected to star number by Hanneke 2024. For binary H: eluder dim ≤ VCdim. ROLE: Characterizes active learning tetrachotomy (Hanneke 2024).
nodes.formal_definition: Threshold dimension of version spaces. Controls active learning query complexity. Introduced in Russo-Van Roy 2013 for RL, connected to star number by Hanneke 2024. For binary H: eluder dim ≤ VCdim.

provenance.note: Russo-Van Roy 2013 for RL, Hanneke 2024 for connection to star number

nodes.name: TeachingDimension
nodes.description: DEFINITION: TD(c, C) = min{|T| : T uniquely identifies c in C}. The minimum number of labeled examples needed for an optimal teacher to teach c to any consistent learner. TD(C) = max_{c∈C} TD(c,C). CAN BE MUCH LARGER THAN VCdim. CONNECTION: Teaching = verification. The teacher provides a 'proof' that c is the target.
nodes.formal_definition: TD(c, C) = min{|T| : T uniquely identifies c in C}. The minimum number of labeled examples needed for an optimal teacher to teach c to any consistent learner. TD(C) = max_{c∈C} TD(c,C). CAN BE MUCH LARGER THAN VCdim.

nodes.name: SampleComplexity
nodes.description: DEFINITION: m(ε,δ) = minimum number of samples for P[error ≤ ε] ≥ 1-δ. BOUNDS: Realizable PAC: Θ(d/ε + log(1/δ)/ε) (Hanneke 2016, optimal). Agnostic PAC: Θ(d/ε² + log(1/δ)/ε²). PAC-Bayes: depends on KL(Q‖P), not d. OPEN: Exact constants for specific classes.
nodes.formal_definition: m(ε,δ) = minimum number of samples for P[error ≤ ε] ≥ 1-δ.
key_theorems: BOUNDS: Realizable PAC: Θ(d/ε + log(1/δ)/ε) (Hanneke 2016, optimal)
open_problems: OPEN: Exact constants for specific classes

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: QueryComplexity
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Number of oracle queries (MQ, EQ, or both) needed to exactly identify target. FOR DFA: O(n²|Σ|) MQs + n EQs via L* (Angluin 1987). CONNECTION TO LDIM: EQ count ≥ Ldim(H) (each EQ eliminates at most half the version space).
nodes.formal_definition: Number of oracle queries (MQ, EQ, or both) needed to exactly identify target.
connections: CONNECTION TO LDIM: EQ count ≥ Ldim(H) (each EQ eliminates at most half the version space)

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: MindChangeCount
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Maximum number of hypothesis revisions before convergence, in worst case. FINITE BOUND: FIN-learning = 0 mind changes. Ex-learning allows finite but unbounded. ORDINAL EXTENSION: MindChangeOrdinal generalizes to transfinite.
nodes.formal_definition: Maximum number of hypothesis revisions before convergence, in worst case. FINITE BOUND: FIN-learning = 0 mind changes. Ex-learning allows finite but unbounded. ORDINAL EXTENSION: MindChangeOrdinal generalizes to transfinite.
key_theorems: FINITE BOUND: FIN-learning = 0 mind changes
Ex-learning allows finite but unbounded

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: MindChangeOrdinal
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Uses constructive ordinals from Kleene's system O as mind-change counters. TxtEx_α: learner carries ordinal counter initialized to α, decremented at each mind change. HIERARCHY: ∀ α<β, TxtEx_α ⊂ TxtEx_β ⊂ TxtEx (all strict). ∪_α TxtEx_α ⊊ TxtEx. EXAMPLE: Unions of n pattern languages have mind-change complexity exactly ω^n. FOUR TYPES (Sharma-Stephan-Ventsov 2004): constant, ordinal, linearly ordered, partially ordered — strict hierarchy.
nodes.formal_definition: Uses constructive ordinals from Kleene's system O as mind-change counters. TxtEx_α: learner carries ordinal counter initialized to α, decremented at each mind change.

nodes.name: OrdinalVCDim
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Martin-Sharma-Stephan 2006): Extension of VC dimension using ordinal-valued ranks on shattered configurations. Reduces to classical VCdim for finite ordinals. MAIN THEOREM: Under Boolean closure, ordinal VCdim perfectly characterizes ordinal-bounded predictive complexity. CONNECTION: Type-level to predictive complexity; see_also (mediated) to mind-change ordinal.
nodes.formal_definition: Extension of VC dimension from natural numbers to countable ordinals (Martin-Sharma-Stephan, ALT 2003/TCS 2006). Replaces 'largest shattered set of size d' with ordinal-valued rank on shattered configurations. When ordinal is finite n, coincides with VCdim = n. Main theorem: under Boolean closure of the domain, ordinal VCdim perfectly characterizes ordinal-bounded predictive complexity.
key_theorems: MAIN THEOREM: Under Boolean closure, ordinal VCdim perfectly characterizes ordinal-bounded predictive complexity
connections: CONNECTION: Type-level to predictive complexity; see_also (mediated) to mind-change ordinal

nodes.name: MistakeBound
nodes.description: DEFINITION: M = max over all adversarial sequences of total prediction errors. OPTIMAL: M = Ldim(H) for realizable online learning. ALGORITHMS: SOA achieves Ldim(H). Halving achieves log₂|H|. Weighted majority: O(Ldim(H)·log|H|). ROLE: The online analog of sample complexity.
nodes.formal_definition: M = max over all adversarial sequences of total prediction errors. OPTIMAL: M = Ldim(H) for realizable online learning. ALGORITHMS: SOA achieves Ldim(H). Halving achieves log₂|H|. Weighted majority: O(Ldim(H)·log|H|).

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: GeneralizationError
nodes.description: DEFINITION: R(h) = E_{(x,y)∼D}[ℓ(h(x),y)] — expected loss on fresh samples from D. For 0-1 loss: R(h) = P[h(x) ≠ y]. EMPIRICAL: R̂(h) = (1/n)Σℓ(h(x_i),y_i). GENERALIZATION GAP: gen(h) = R(h) - R̂(h). All generalization bounds (VC, PAC-Bayes, Rademacher, stability, info-theoretic) bound this gap.
nodes.formal_definition: R(h) = E_{(x,y)∼D}[ℓ(h(x),y)] — expected loss on fresh samples from D. For 0-1 loss: R(h) = P[h(x) ≠ y]. EMPIRICAL: R̂(h) = (1/n)Σℓ(h(x_i),y_i). GENERALIZATION GAP: gen(h) = R(h) - R̂(h). All generalization bounds (VC, PAC-Bayes, Rademacher, stability, info-theoretic) bound this gap.
key_theorems: All generalization bounds (VC, PAC-Bayes, Rademacher, stability, info-theoretic) bound this gap

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: Regret
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Regret_T = Σ_{t=1}^T ℓ(ĥ_t, z_t) - min_{h∈H} Σ_{t=1}^T ℓ(h, z_t). Cumulative loss minus best-in-class cumulative loss. MINIMAX OPTIMAL: For finite H: Θ(√(T·log|H|)). For real-valued with Ldim=d: Θ(√(d·T)). NO-REGRET: Regret_T/T → 0 ↔ average loss approaches best fixed hypothesis.
nodes.formal_definition: Regret_T = Σ_{t=1}^T ℓ(ĥ_t, z_t) - min_{h∈H} Σ_{t=1}^T ℓ(h, z_t). Cumulative loss minus best-in-class cumulative loss.
key_theorems: NO-REGRET: Regret_T/T → 0 ↔ average loss approaches best fixed hypothesis

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: LabelComplexity
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Number of labels an active learner needs to request. The active learning analog of sample complexity. KEY RESULT: For thresholds on [0,1], passive needs O(1/ε), active needs O(log(1/ε)) — exponential improvement. DISAGREEMENT COEFFICIENT: θ = lim_{ε→0} P(DIS(B(h*,ε)))/ε controls label complexity of CAL.
nodes.formal_definition: Number of labels an active learner needs to request. The active learning analog of sample complexity.

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: DescriptionLength
nodes.description: DEFINITION: |d(h)| = bits needed to encode hypothesis h under coding scheme d. In MDL: select h minimizing |d(h)| + |d(data|h)|. In Occam's razor: short description → generalization. CONNECTION: Kolmogorov complexity K(h) = min description length over all programs.
nodes.formal_definition: |d(h)| = bits needed to encode hypothesis h under coding scheme d. In MDL: select h minimizing |d(h)| + |d(data|h)|. In Occam's razor: short description → generalization.
connections: CONNECTION: Kolmogorov complexity K(h) = min description length over all programs

provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: KolmogorovComplexity
nodes.description: DEFINITION: K(x) = min{|p| : U(p) = x} — length of shortest program on universal TM U that outputs x. UNCOMPUTABLE: K(x) is not computable (proof via Berry paradox / diagonalization). ROLE IN LEARNING: Solomonoff's universal prior M(x) = Σ 2^{-|p|} over programs outputting x. MDL and MML approximate K. Li-Tromp-Vitányi 2003: Replace description length with K in Occam bound for tighter results.
nodes.formal_definition: K(x) = min{|p| : U(p) = x} — length of shortest program on universal TM U that outputs x. UNCOMPUTABLE: K(x) is not computable (proof via Berry paradox / diagonalization). ROLE IN LEARNING: Solomonoff's universal prior M(x) = Σ 2^{-|p|} over programs outputting x. MDL and MML approximate K. provenance.field_origin: See node references and description.

nodes.name: DriftRate
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Δ = max_t P_{x∼D}[c_t(x) ≠ c_{t+1}(x)] — probability target changes between consecutive time steps. HELMBOLD-LONG 1994: Tracking error ≈ O((d·Δ)^{1/3}) where d = VCdim. LONG 1999: This is tight. ROLE: The key parameter for non-stationary learning. Low Δ = slow drift = tracking feasible.
nodes.formal_definition: Δ = max_t P_{x∼D}[c_t(x) ≠ c_{t+1}(x)] — probability target changes between consecutive time steps.

nodes.name: GoldsTheorem
nodes.description: STATEMENT (Gold 1967): Any superfinite class (containing all finite languages + at least one infinite language) is NOT identifiable in the limit from text (positive data only). PROOF IDEA: Diagonalization. For any learner M, construct a language L that defeats M by mimicking a finite language long enough to force M to converge, then extending L to invalidate the convergence. The constraint is on the data channel, not the learner.
nodes.formal_definition: Any class C of languages that contains all finite languages and at least one infinite language is NOT identifiable in the limit from text (positive data only).
nodes.proof_sketch: Proof: diagonalization. For any learner M, construct language L that mimics a finite language long enough to force M to converge to a finite-language hypothesis, then extend L beyond that hypothesis, forcing M to be wrong.

nodes.name: NFLTheorem
nodes.description: STATEMENT (Wolpert-Macready 1997): For any learner A, averaged over ALL target functions f: X → Y (uniform over Y^X), A has the same expected performance as random guessing. Formally: Σ_f R_D(A(S),f) = constant ∀ A. IMPLICATION: Without assumptions (inductive bias), learning is impossible. The choice of hypothesis space IS the inductive bias. Learning requires identity constraints.
nodes.formal_definition: No Free Lunch (Wolpert-Macready 1997). For any learner A, averaged over ALL target functions f uniformly: Σ_f R(A(S),f) = constant, independent of A. The choice of hypothesis space IS the inductive bias.

nodes.name: PACLowerBound
nodes.description: STATEMENT: For any learner, ∃ target in C with VCdim(C)=d and distribution D s.t. if m < d/(32ε), then P[R(h) > ε] > 1/8. More precisely: m ≥ Ω(d/ε + log(1/δ)/ε) is NECESSARY for PAC learning. PROOF: Probabilistic argument — choose random target from 2^d options on a shattered set, show learner can't distinguish them with too few samples
nodes.formal_definition: For any learner, ∃ target in C with VCdim(C)=d and distribution D s.t. if m < d/(32ε), then P[R(h) > ε] > 1/8. More precisely: m ≥ Ω(d/ε + log(1/δ)/ε) is NECESSARY for PAC learning.

nodes.name: VCCharacterization
nodes.description: STATEMENT: VCdim(H) < ∞ ↔ H is PAC learnable. This is one direction of the fundamental theorem. The full equivalence includes 7 other conditions. QUANTITATIVE: VCdim=d ⟹ sample complexity Θ(d/ε) (realizable, optimal)

nodes.name: LittlestoneCharacterization
nodes.description: STATEMENT: Ldim(H) < ∞ ↔ H is online learnable with finite mistake bound. Optimal mistake bound = Ldim(H). Also: Ldim(H) < ∞ ↔ H is DP-PAC learnable (Alon et al. 2019). One-sided: VCdim ≤ Ldim always. No reverse.

nodes.name: MindChangeCharacterization
nodes.description: STATEMENT: The ordinal mind-change hierarchy is strict: ∀ constructive ordinals α<β, TxtEx_α ⊂ TxtEx_β ⊂ TxtEx. The mind-change ordinal of a class characterizes its position in this hierarchy
nodes.formal_definition: The ordinal mind-change hierarchy is strict: ∀ constructive ordinals α<β, TxtEx_α ⊂ TxtEx_β ⊂ TxtEx. The mind-change ordinal of a class characterizes its position in this hierarchy

nodes.name: UniversalTrichotomy
nodes.description: STATEMENT (Bousquet et al. 2021): For |H|≥3, exactly one of: (1) No infinite Littlestone tree → optimal universal rate e^{−n}. (2) Infinite Littlestone tree, no infinite VCL tree → rate 1/n. (3) Infinite VCL tree → arbitrarily slow. EXTENSIONS: Agnostic tetrachotomy (Hanneke-Moran 2026). Active learning tetrachotomy (Hanneke 2024)

nodes.name: AdviceReduction
nodes.description: STATEMENT: Advice (auxiliary information) can reduce sample complexity by constraining the effective hypothesis space. But robust learning (succeeding with EVERY oracle of given degree) = unaided learning. The TYPE of advice matters more than computational power

nodes.name: MetaPACBound
nodes.description: STATEMENT (Baxter 2000): Total error ≈ O(complexity(H_λ)/m + complexity(Λ)/n) where m = samples per task, n = tasks. When n=1, |Λ|=1: reduces to standard PAC

nodes.name: DFA
nodes.description: DEFINITION: (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) where Q = finite states, Σ = alphabet, δ: Q×Σ → Q = transition function, q₀ ∈ Q = start, F ⊆ Q = accepting. Accepts L(A) = {w ∈ Σ* : δ*(q₀,w) ∈ F}. LEARNING: Exactly learnable with MQ+EQ (Angluin L*, 1987). NP-hard to learn from examples alone (Gold 1978). STATE COMPLEXITY: Minimum DFA for L has unique size (Myhill-Nerode).
nodes.formal_definition: (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) where Q = finite states, Σ = alphabet, δ: Q×Σ → Q = transition function, q₀ ∈ Q = start, F ⊆ Q = accepting. Accepts L(A) = {w ∈ Σ* : δ*(q₀,w) ∈ F}. nodes.name: NFA
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Like DFA but δ: Q×Σ → P(Q) (set-valued). Accepts if ANY path leads to accepting state. EQUIVALENCE: Every NFA has equivalent DFA (subset construction), possibly with 2^|Q| states. LEARNING: NFA learning is harder than DFA (exponential blowup in state space).
nodes.formal_definition: NFA = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) where δ: Q×Σ → P(Q). Accepts w if ANY computation path leads to accepting state. Equivalent to DFA (subset construction, possibly exponential blowup).
key_theorems: EQUIVALENCE: Every NFA has equivalent DFA (subset construction), possibly with 2^|Q| states

provenance.field_origin: Standard computer science / formal language theory.

nodes.name: RegularLanguage
nodes.description: DEFINITION: L ⊆ Σ* recognized by some DFA. Equivalently: described by regular expression, or generated by right-linear grammar. Closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, Kleene star. LEARNING: L* learns regular languages from MQ+EQ. Gold's theorem: not identifiable from text alone (since regular languages form a superfinite class).

nodes.name: ContextFreeGrammar
nodes.description: DEFINITION: G = (V, Σ, R, S) where V = variables, Σ = terminals, R ⊆ V × (V∪Σ)* = productions, S ∈ V = start. Each production A → α replaces one variable by a string. LEARNING: Sakakibara 1992: subclass of CFGs learnable from structural examples. General CFGs not learnable from text (Gold).

nodes.name: ContextFreeLanguage

nodes.name: PushdownAutomaton
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Finite automaton + stack. Formally: (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, Z₀, F) where Γ = stack alphabet, δ: Q×(Σ∪{ε})×Γ → P(Q×Γ*). The stack provides unbounded LIFO memory. Nondeterministic PDAs recognize exactly the context-free languages.

nodes.name: TuringMachine
nodes.description: DEFINITION: (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject) with infinite tape. Can compute any computable function. Church-Turing thesis: TMs capture 'effectively computable'. ROLE IN LEARNING: Learners modeled as TMs (computability constraints). Gold's model: learner = TM outputting hypothesis indices. Kearns-Valiant: hardness via reduction to TM problems.

nodes.name: PartialComputableFunction
nodes.description: DEFINITION: f: ℕ ⇀ ℕ computed by some TM that may not halt on all inputs. The domain dom(f) = {n : TM halts on n}. Equivalently: partial recursive function. ROLE: Hypothesis spaces in Gold's model are often indexed by partial recursive functions. The learner outputs an index e such that φ_e = target.

nodes.name: GodelNumbering
nodes.description: DEFINITION: An effective bijection between programs and natural numbers. Each natural number e encodes a program/TM, and φ_e denotes the partial function it computes. ROLE: In Gold's model, the learner outputs Gödel numbers (hypothesis indices). The numbering must be effective (there's a universal TM that simulates φ_e on input x given e,x).

nodes.name: RecursivelyEnumerableSet
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A ⊆ ℕ is recursively enumerable if ∃ TM that halts and accepts on exactly the members of A (may diverge on non-members). Equivalently: A = dom(φ_e) for some e. ROLE: Many concept classes in learning theory are r.e. sets of descriptions. A set is decidable iff both it and its complement are r.e.

nodes.name: LimitingRecursion
nodes.description: DEFINITION: f is limiting recursive if ∃ computable g s.t. g(x,t) → f(x) as t → ∞ (stabilizes for each x). Equivalently: f ∈ Δ⁰₂. ROLE: Ex-learning IS limiting recursion on hypothesis indices. Gold's identification in the limit = trial-and-error computation.

nodes.name: Delta02Class
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Δ⁰₂ = sets decidable by trial-and-error (limit computable). A ∈ Δ⁰₂ iff A = {x : lim_{t→∞} g(x,t) = 1} for computable g. Equivalently: A is the difference of two r.e. sets. ROLE: The computability-theoretic complexity class of problems solvable by identification in the limit.

nodes.name: AlgorithmicProbability
nodes.description: DEFINITION: M(x) = Σ_{p: U(p)=x} 2^{-|p|} — sum of 2^{-|p|} over all programs producing x. Solomonoff's universal prior. PROPERTIES: Uncomputable but dominates every computable distribution (up to multiplicative constant). Theoretically optimal for sequence prediction. ROLE: The 'ideal' inductive bias. MDL and MML are computable approximations.

nodes.name: MinimumDescriptionLength
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Select hypothesis minimizing |d(h)| + |d(data|h)| — description of model + description of data given model. RISSANEN 1978. Formally: MDL is a two-part code. Practical: normalized maximum likelihood (NML). CONNECTION: Approximation of Kolmogorov complexity. MDL → Occam's razor theorem when applied to PAC.

nodes.name: MinimumMessageLength
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Wallace-Boulton 1968): Bayesian counterpart of MDL. Minimize total message length = |encode(prior info about h)| + |encode(data|h)|. DISTINCTION FROM MDL: MML is Bayesian (explicit prior), MDL is frequentist (code-based). Both embody Occam's razor. CONVERGENCE: Under regularity conditions, MML converges to true model at optimal rate.

nodes.name: BayesianInference
nodes.description: DEFINITION: P(θ|data) ∝ P(data|θ)·P(θ). Sequential update: posterior after seeing x₁,...,x_n becomes prior for x_{n+1}. ROLE IN LEARNING: Bayesian learner = sequential posterior update. PAC-Bayes = frequentist analysis of Bayesian-style algorithms. INDUCTIVE BIAS: The prior P(θ) IS the inductive bias, made explicit.

nodes.name: StructuralRiskMinimization
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Vapnik 1998): Organize hypotheses into nested classes H₁ ⊂ H₂ ⊂ ... of increasing complexity. Select k minimizing empirical_risk(H_k) + complexity_penalty(H_k). IMPLEMENTATION: Penalty ∝ √(VCdim(H_k)/n). Balances fit vs complexity. EXAMPLE: SVM with different kernel widths. Neural nets with different layer counts. ROLE: Practical implementation of Occam's razor.

nodes.name: GrammarInduction
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Infer a formal grammar (DFA, CFG, etc.) from examples of its language. GOLD 1967: Superfinite classes not learnable from text. ANGLUIN 1987: DFAs learnable with MQ+EQ (L*). DE LA HIGUERA 1997: Characteristic sets for polynomial grammatical inference. APPLICATIONS: NLP, DNA modeling, protocol inference.

nodes.name: LStarAlgorithm
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Angluin 1987): Exactly learns minimal DFA from membership + equivalence queries. Maintains observation table (S, E, T). Extends S and E using counterexamples from EQ. Queries: O(n²|Σ|) MQs, n EQs where n = states in minimal DFA. ROLE: The canonical active learning algorithm. Demonstrates power of query access over passive data.

nodes.name: CEGIS
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Counterexample-Guided Inductive Synthesis. Loop: (1) Synthesizer proposes candidate, (2) Verifier checks against spec, (3) If fail: counterexample fed back to synthesizer, (4) Repeat. CONVERGENCE: Guaranteed with crisp verifier (each counterexample eliminates hypotheses). BREAKS with fuzzy oracle (LLM, reward model) — convergence proof requires exact counterexamples.

nodes.name: ConceptDrift
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Target concept changes over time. Formally: sequence c₁, c₂,... with c_t potentially ≠ c_{t+1}. DRIFT RATE: Δ = max_t P[c_t(x) ≠ c_{t+1}(x)]. TRACKING: Error ≈ O((d·Δ)^{1/3}). TYPES: Sudden (abrupt change), gradual (slow transition), recurring (cycle). ROLE: Motivates continual/lifelong learning. Standard convergence guarantees fail under drift.

nodes.name: InductiveLogicProgramming

Output: hypothesis H s.t. B∧H ⊨ E+ and B∧H ⊭ e for e∈E-. MUGGLETON 1991: PROGOL system. APPLICATIONS: Drug discovery, knowledge extraction.

CEGIS = active learning with equivalence oracle.

nodes.name: ExecutionTrace

nodes.name: PartialTrace
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Incomplete execution trace — a prefix or fragment of a full trace. LEARNING: From partial traces, the learner must infer the full program. This is harder than learning from complete traces. BIERMANN-KRISHNASWAMY 1976: Constructing programs from example computations.

nodes.name: BayesianLearner
nodes.description: DEFINITION: A learner whose state is a probability distribution over H, updated via Bayes' rule. Formally: State = Distribution(H), update(P, (x,y)) = P(·|x,y) via Bayes. OUTPUT: MAP hypothesis (mode of posterior) or full posterior distribution. CONNECTION TO PAC-BAYES: PAC-Bayes analyzes generalization of the posterior distribution. The prior P in PAC-Bayes is the Bayesian learner's initial state.
