nodes.name: PosteriorConsistency
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Posterior concentrates on true parameter as n→∞. Formally: ∀ ε>0, P(θ∈N_ε(θ*)|X₁,...,X_n) → 1, P_{θ*}^∞-a.s. DOOB 1949: Holds for Π-almost-all θ* (but exception set can be large). SCHWARTZ 1965: KL support condition (Π({p : KL(p₀‖p) ≤ ε}) > 0 ∀ε>0) suffices for weak consistency. GHOSAL-GHOSH-VDV 2000: Rate ε̄_n via prior mass + sieve entropy conditions. For α-smooth densities: ε̄_n ≈ n^{−α/(2α+1)}.
nodes.formal_definition: Posterior concentrates on true parameter as n→∞. Doob 1949: holds for Π-almost-all θ* (but exception set can be topologically large). Schwartz 1965: sufficient condition is KL support: Π({p : KL(p₀‖p) ≤ ε}) > 0 for all ε > 0. Rate (Ghosal-Ghosh-van der Vaart 2000): ε̄_n via prior mass condition Π(B(ε̃_n)) ≥ exp(-Cnε̄_n²) and sieve entropy condition. For α-smooth densities: ε̄_n ≈ n^{-α/(2α+1)}.

nodes.name: PACBayesBound
nodes.description: DEFINITION: For prior P on H, with prob ≥ 1-δ over S of size n, simultaneously ∀ posterior Q: E_{h∼Q}[R(h)] ≤ E_{h∼Q}[R̂(h)] + √((KL(Q‖P) + ln(2√n/δ))/(2n)). MCALLESTER 1998/1999. TIGHTER: Seeger-Maurer kl-bound: kl(R̂_Q ‖ R_Q) ≤ (KL(Q‖P) + ln(2√n/δ))/n. CATONI 2007: Free parameter λ yields tightest bounds. Optimal Q = Gibbs posterior Q_λ ∝ exp(-λR̂)P. TYPE IMPACT: Changes learner output from single h to distribution Q over H. KL(Q‖P) replaces VCdim as complexity.
nodes.formal_definition: For any prior P on H fixed before seeing data, any δ ∈ (0,1), with probability ≥ 1-δ over sample S of size n, simultaneously for all posteriors Q: E_{h∼Q}[R(h)] ≤ E_{h∼Q}[R̂(h)] + √((KL(Q‖P) + ln(2√n/δ))/(2n)). McAllester 1998/1999. Tighter: kl(R̂_Q ‖ R_Q) ≤ (KL(Q‖P) + ln(2√n/δ))/n (Seeger 2002, Maurer 2004). Optimal Q = Gibbs posterior Q_λ ∝ exp(-λR̂)P (Catoni 2007).
key_theorems: TIGHTER: Seeger-Maurer kl-bound: kl(R̂_Q ‖ R_Q) ≤ (KL(Q‖P) + ln(2√n/δ))/n
CATONI 2007: Free parameter λ yields tightest bounds

nodes.name: GibbsPosterior
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Q_λ ∝ exp(-λ·R̂(h))·P — the distribution minimizing the PAC-Bayes bound. PARAMETER λ > 0 controls temperature: λ→0 gives prior P, λ→∞ gives ERM. CATONI 2007: Called this 'thermodynamics of statistical learning'. OPTIMALITY: For any PAC-Bayes bound, the Gibbs posterior with tuned λ achieves the tightest bound over all posteriors Q.
nodes.formal_definition: Q_λ ∝ exp(-λ·R̂(h))·P — the distribution minimizing the PAC-Bayes bound.
key_theorems: OPTIMALITY: For any PAC-Bayes bound, the Gibbs posterior with tuned λ achieves the tightest bound over all posteriors Q
remarks: DEFINITION: Q_λ ∝ exp(-λ·R̂(h))·P — the distribution minimizing the PAC-Bayes bound

nodes.name: KLComplexity
nodes.description: DEFINITION: KL(Q‖P) = E_Q[ln(dQ/dP)] — Kullback-Leibler divergence from prior P to posterior Q. ROLE IN PAC-BAYES: Replaces VC dimension as complexity measure. Measures how much the learned distribution deviates from the prior. KL=0 means Q=P (no learning). KL→∞ means Q concentrates on a single h (full commitment). CATONI: Called KL the 'thermodynamic potential' of learning.
nodes.formal_definition: KL(Q‖P) = E_Q[ln(dQ/dP)] — Kullback-Leibler divergence from prior P to posterior Q. ROLE IN PAC-BAYES: Replaces VC dimension as complexity measure. Measures how much the learned distribution deviates from the prior. KL=0 means Q=P (no learning). KL→∞ means Q concentrates on a single h (full commitment).

nodes.name: CompressionScheme
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Compression map κ takes labeled sample to subsequence of size ≤ k (+ auxiliary info). Reconstruction map ρ recovers hypothesis consistent with full sample. Size = k + log|aux|. MORAN-YEHUDAYOFF 2016: VCdim=d ⟹ compression of size 2^{O(d)}. CONJECTURE (Littlestone-Warmuth 1986): Compression of size O(d). STILL OPEN. Occam's razor: compression of size k → PAC learning with O(k/ε) samples.
nodes.formal_definition: Compression map κ takes labeled sample to subsequence of size ≤ k (+ auxiliary info). Reconstruction map ρ recovers hypothesis consistent with full sample. Size = k + log|aux|.

nodes.name: AlgorithmicStability
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Bousquet-Elisseeff 2002): Algorithm A has uniform stability β if ∀ S, ∀i, sup_z |ℓ(A_S,z) - ℓ(A_{S\i},z)| ≤ β. GENERALIZATION BOUND: |R(A_S) - R̂(A_S)| ≤ 2β + (4mβ+M)√(ln(1/δ)/(2m)). FOR REGULARIZATION: Tikhonov with λ gives β = σ²κ²/(2mλ). DEEPEST RESULT (Shalev-Shwartz et al. 2010): Beyond binary classification, learnability ⟺ stable AERM. Stability replaces uniform convergence as the fundamental characterization.
nodes.formal_definition: Uniform stability β (Bousquet-Elisseeff 2002): ∀ training sets S, ∀ index i, sup_z |ℓ(A_S,z) - ℓ(A_{S\i},z)| ≤ β where S\i = S with i-th example removed. Generalization bound: |R(A_S) - R̂(A_S)| ≤ 2β + (4mβ+M)√(ln(1/δ)/(2m)). For Tikhonov regularization with parameter λ: β = σ²κ²/(2mλ). Deepest result: beyond binary 0-1 loss, learnability ↔ stable AERM (Shalev-Shwartz et al. 2010).
key_theorems: GENERALIZATION BOUND: |R(A_S) - R̂(A_S)| ≤ 2β + (4mβ+M)√(ln(1/δ)/(2m))
2010): Beyond binary classification, learnability ⟺ stable AERM
Stability replaces uniform convergence as the fundamental characterization

nodes.name: RademacherComplexity
nodes.description: DEFINITION: R̂_n(G) = E_σ[sup_{g∈G} (1/n)Σσ_i·g(z_i)] where σ_i ∈ {±1} uniform. Rademacher complexity R_n(G) = E_S[R̂_n(G)]. GENERALIZATION BOUND (Bartlett-Mendelson 2002): ∀g∈G, E[g] ≤ (1/n)Σg(z_i) + 2R_n(G) + c√(ln(1/δ)/n). CONNECTION TO VC: R_n(H) ≤ √(2d·ln(en/d)/n) via Massart + Sauer-Shelah. DATA-DEPENDENT: R̂_n uses the actual sample, yielding tighter bounds than worst-case. CALCULUS: R(conv F) = R(F). R(φ∘F) ≤ 2L·R(F) for L-Lipschitz φ.
nodes.formal_definition: Empirical Rademacher complexity: R̂_n(G) = E_σ[sup_{g∈G} (1/n)Σ_{i=1}^n σ_i·g(z_i)] where σ_i ∈ {±1} i.i.d. uniform. Population: R_n(G) = E_S[R̂_n(G)]. Generalization bound (Bartlett-Mendelson 2002): for G ⊆ [0,1]^Z, with prob ≥ 1-δ, ∀g∈G: E[g] ≤ (1/n)Σg(z_i) + 2R_n(G) + √(ln(1/δ)/(2n)). Connection to VC: R_n(H) ≤ √(2d·ln(en/d)/n) via Massart lemma + Sauer-Shelah.
key_theorems: GENERALIZATION BOUND (Bartlett-Mendelson 2002): ∀g∈G, E[g] ≤ (1/n)Σg(z_i) + 2R_n(G) + c√(ln(1/δ)/n)
DATA-DEPENDENT: R̂_n uses the actual sample, yielding tighter bounds than worst-case
connections: CONNECTION TO VC: R_n(H) ≤ √(2d·ln(en/d)/n) via Massart + Sauer-Shelah

nodes.name: InformationTheoreticBound
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Xu-Raginsky 2017): If ℓ(w,Z) is σ-sub-Gaussian ∀w, then |E[gen(W,S)]| ≤ √(2σ²I(S;W)/n) where I(S;W) = mutual information between training set and algorithm output. STEINKE-ZAKYNTHINOU 2020: CMI framework: I(A(Z̃_S);S|Z̃) ≤ n·log 2 always finite. Unifies PAC-Bayes, VC, stability. BU-ZOU-VEERAVALLI 2020: Per-sample MI: |gen| ≤ (1/n)Σ√(2σ_i²·I(W;Z_i)). Always tighter.
nodes.formal_definition: Xu-Raginsky 2017: If loss ℓ(w,Z) is σ-sub-Gaussian under Z∼μ for all w, then |E[gen(W,S)]| ≤ √(2σ²I(S;W)/n) where I(S;W) = mutual information between training set and algorithm output. CMI framework (Steinke-Zakynthinou 2020): replaces I(W;S) with conditional mutual information I(A(Z̃_S);S|Z̃) which is always ≤ n·log 2. Per-sample version (Bu-Zou-Veeravalli 2020): |gen| ≤ (1/n)Σ√(2σ²_i·I(W;Z_i)).

nodes.name: GrangerCausality
nodes.note: Included for completeness. Not in formalization kernel — no new types needed.
nodes.description: DEFINITION: X Granger-causes Y iff past X improves prediction of Y beyond past Y alone. Formally: E[Y_t|Y_{<t}, X_{<t}] ≠ E[Y_t|Y_{<t}]. MECHANISM: F-test on VAR coefficients (linear). Neural Granger (Tank et al. 2021) for nonlinear. ASSUMPTIONS: Stationarity, complete system (no unmeasured confounders), known lag order. ROLE IN GRAPH: Application of online prediction + hypothesis testing. No new types needed for the formalization kernel.
nodes.formal_definition: X Granger-causes Y iff past X improves prediction of Y beyond past Y alone. Formally: E[Y_t|Y_{<t}, X_{<t}] ≠ E[Y_t|Y_{<t}].

provenance.field_origin: Applied methodology. See node description for references.

remarks: DEFINITION: X Granger-causes Y iff past X improves prediction of Y beyond past Y alone

nodes.name: Sauer-Shelah Lemma
nodes.description: STATEMENT: Let H be a hypothesis class with VCdim(H) = d. Define the growth function Π_H(n) = max_{|C|=n} |{h|_C : h ∈ H}|. Then for all n: Π_H(n) ≤ Σ_{i=0}^d C(n,i). For n ≥ d: Π_H(n) ≤ (en/d)^d. DICHOTOMY: If VCdim = ∞ then Π_H(n) = 2^n for all n. If VCdim = d < ∞ then Π_H(n) = O(n^d). No intermediate behavior exists. PROOF IDEA: Induction on n. Split H|_C into pairs that agree/disagree on x_n. Disagreeing pairs have VCdim ≤ d-1. Apply Pascal's identity. ROLE: Critical for proving uniform convergence in PAC bounds. Feeds into Rademacher complexity via Massart's lemma: R_n(H) ≤ √(2d·ln(en/d)/n). Tight on extremal classes (all subsets of size < d).
nodes.formal_definition: Let H be a hypothesis class with VCdim(H) = d. Define the growth function Π_H(n) = max_{|C|=n} |{h|_C : h ∈ H}|. Then for all n: Π_H(n) ≤ Σ_{i=0}^d C(n,i). For n ≥ d: Π_H(n) ≤ (en/d)^d.
remarks: ROLE: Critical for proving uniform convergence in PAC bounds

nodes.name: Fundamental Theorem of Statistical Learning
nodes.description: STATEMENT: For binary hypothesis class H with 0-1 loss, the following are equivalent: (i) VCdim(H) < ∞. (ii) Uniform convergence: ∀ε,δ ∃m s.t. P[sup_{h∈H}|R(h)-R̂(h)| ≤ ε] ≥ 1-δ. (iii) Any ERM is a successful agnostic PAC learner. (iv) H is agnostic PAC learnable. (v) H is PAC learnable (realizable). (vi) Any ERM succeeds (realizable). (vii) H has a finite sample compression scheme (Moran-Yehudayoff 2016). (viii) Empirical Rademacher complexity R̂_n(H) → 0. (ix) H is uniform Glivenko-Cantelli. SAMPLE COMPLEXITY: Agnostic: Θ(d/ε² + log(1/δ)/ε²). Realizable ERM: Θ(d·log(1/ε)/ε + log(1/δ)/ε). Realizable optimal (Hanneke 2016): Θ(d/ε + log(1/δ)/ε) via improper majority vote. CRITICAL CAVEAT: Beyond binary 0-1 loss, this equivalence BREAKS. Shalev-Shwartz et al. 2010: learnability ⟺ stable AERM, NOT uniform convergence. Stability replaces VC dimension as the fundamental characterization in general settings.
nodes.formal_definition: For binary hypothesis class H with 0-1 loss, the following are equivalent: (i) VCdim(H) < ∞. (ii) Uniform convergence: ∀ε,δ ∃m s.t. P[sup_{h∈H}|R(h)-R̂(h)| ≤ ε] ≥ 1-δ. (iii) Any ERM is a successful agnostic PAC learner. (iv) H is agnostic PAC learnable. (v) H is PAC learnable (realizable). (vi) Any ERM succeeds (realizable). (vii) H has a finite sample compression scheme (Moran-Yehudayoff 2016). (viii) Empirical Rademacher complexity R̂_n(H) → 0. (ix) H is uniform Glivenko-Cantelli.
key_theorems: STATEMENT: For binary hypothesis class H with 0-1 loss, the following are equivalent: (i) VCdim(H) < ∞
CRITICAL CAVEAT: Beyond binary 0-1 loss, this equivalence BREAKS
2010: learnability ⟺ stable AERM, NOT uniform convergence
Stability replaces VC dimension as the fundamental characterization in general settings

provenance.note: Assembled across multiple papers 1971-2016. Compression direction: Moran-Yehudayoff 2016. Optimal sample complexity: Hanneke 2016.

nodes.name: Proper vs Improper Computational Separation
nodes.description: STATEMENT (Pitt-Valiant 1988): For k ≥ 2, k-term DNF is not properly PAC learnable unless RP = NP. PROOF: Reduction from k-colorability. For graph G=(V,E): vertex i → positive example with x_j=1 for j≠i, x_i=0. Edge (i,j) → negative example with x_ℓ=1 for ℓ∉{i,j}. Consistent k-term DNF exists iff G is k-colorable (each term = color class). CONTRAST: k-term DNF ⊆ k-CNF, and k-CNF is efficiently PAC learnable (enumerate and filter clauses). So 3-term DNF is NP-hard proper, poly-time improper. SAMPLE COMPLEXITY: No separation in standard PAC (both Θ(d/ε)). Separation exists in DP-PAC: proper can need Ω(log|X|) more samples (Beimel et al. 2013). TYPE IMPACT: Output space of Learner must be parameterized independently of ConceptClass — proper flag is type-level.
nodes.formal_definition: (Pitt-Valiant 1988): For k ≥ 2, k-term DNF is not properly PAC learnable unless RP = NP.
key_theorems: Consistent k-term DNF exists iff G is k-colorable (each term = color class)

nodes.name: Cryptographic Hardness of Learning
nodes.description: STATEMENT (Kearns-Valiant 1994): Under the Discrete Cube Root Assumption (DCRA), ∃ polynomial P(·) s.t. circuits of size ≤ P(n) are not PAC learnable in poly time by any poly-evaluatable hypothesis class. MECHANISM: RSA decryption f⁻¹_N is a poly-size circuit. PAC learner given (x, f⁻¹_N(x)) could approximate f⁻¹_N, breaking RSA. The concept class has finite VC dim O(s·log s). BARRIER (Applebaum-Barak-Xiao 2008): Cannot prove 'P≠NP ⟹ hard PAC learning' via Karp reductions — average-case/crypto assumptions are INHERENTLY necessary. HIERARCHY: OWF (weakest) → factoring → DCRA → LWE (strongest). Each yields hardness for different classes. SQ MODEL: Parities have VCdim=n but SQdim=2^n. This is UNCONDITIONAL (no crypto). Any SQ algorithm needs ≥ 2^{n/3} queries. Yet parities are learnable in poly-time via Gaussian elimination (not an SQ algorithm).
nodes.formal_definition: (Kearns-Valiant 1994): Under the Discrete Cube Root Assumption (DCRA), ∃ polynomial P(·) s.t. circuits of size ≤ P(n) are not PAC learnable in poly time by any poly-evaluatable hypothesis class.

nodes.name: Statistical Query Dimension
nodes.description: DEFINITION: SQ-DIM(C,D) = largest d s.t. ∃ f₁,...,f_d ∈ C with |E_{x∼D}[f_i(x)·f_j(x)]| ≤ 1/d for all i≠j. THEOREM (Blum et al. 1994): Lower: any SQ algorithm with tolerance ≥ 1/d^{1/3} needs ≥ d^{1/3}-2 queries for weak learning. Upper: d queries with tolerance 1/(4d) suffice. CANONICAL SEPARATION: Parities over {0,1}^n under uniform distribution: VCdim = n, SQ-DIM = 2^n (all 2^n parities are pairwise uncorrelated: E[χ_S·χ_T] = 0 for S≠T). This is the sharpest known separation between a combinatorial dimension and an algorithmic complexity measure. CONNECTION TO PAC: SQ-learnable ⟹ noise-tolerant PAC learnable (simulation via sampling). But not all PAC-learnable classes are SQ-learnable (parities witness this).
nodes.formal_definition: SQ-DIM(C,D) = largest d s.t. ∃ f₁,...,f_d ∈ C with |E_{x∼D}[f_i(x)·f_j(x)]| ≤ 1/d for all i≠j.
connections: CONNECTION TO PAC: SQ-learnable ⟹ noise-tolerant PAC learnable (simulation via sampling)

nodes.name: Growth Function
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Π_H(n) = max_{C⊆X, |C|=n} |{(h(x₁),...,h(x_n)) : h ∈ H}|. Also called the shatter coefficient or shattering number. PROPERTIES: Π_H(n) ≤ 2^n always. Π_H(n) = 2^n for all n ≤ VCdim(H). Sauer-Shelah: if VCdim(H)=d then Π_H(n) ≤ (en/d)^d for n ≥ d. ROLE: Mediates between VC dimension and generalization bounds. The growth function is what directly enters the uniform convergence argument, not VC dimension itself. VCdim is a single number; the growth function is a whole sequence.
nodes.formal_definition: Π_H(n) = max_{C⊆X, |C|=n} |{(h(x₁),...,h(x_n)) : h ∈ H}|. Also called the shatter coefficient or shattering number.
remarks: ROLE: Mediates between VC dimension and generalization bounds

nodes.name: Occam Algorithm / Occam's Razor Theorem
nodes.description: DEFINITION: L is an (α,β)-Occam algorithm for C using H if on input sample of size m from target of description length n, it outputs consistent h with log₂|H_{n,m}| ≤ n^α · m^β, where α ≥ 0 and 0 ≤ β < 1. THEOREM (Blumer et al. 1987): An (α,β)-Occam algorithm PAC-learns with sample complexity m ≥ O((1/ε)log(1/δ) + ((n^α)/ε)^{1/(1-β)}). SIMPLE VERSION: If algorithm outputs consistent h from set of size M, then error ≤ ε w.p. ≥ 1-δ provided m ≥ (1/ε)(ln M + ln(1/δ)). CONNECTIONS: MDL = Occam with description length as complexity. Compression of size k gives effective M = C(m,k)·2^k, feeding into Occam bound. Board-Pitt 1990: partial converse — for polynomially closed classes, PAC ⟹ Occam algorithm exists.
nodes.formal_definition: L is an (α,β)-Occam algorithm for C using H if on input sample of size m from target of description length n, it outputs consistent h with log₂|H_{n,m}| ≤ n^α · m^β, where α ≥ 0 and 0 ≤ β < 1.
key_theorems: Compression of size k gives effective M = C(m,k)·2^k, feeding into Occam bound
connections: CONNECTIONS: MDL = Occam with description length as complexity

nodes.name: Unlabeled Sample Compression
nodes.description: DEFINITION: Unlabeled compression scheme of size k: compression retains only point set S'⊆{x₁,...,x_m} (without labels); reconstruction determines correct concept from point locations alone. POSITIVE: Maximum classes admit unlabeled compression of size d (Kuzmin-Warmuth 2007, Chalopin et al. 2022). NEGATIVE (Pálvölgyi-Tardos 2020): ∃ a VC-2 class with NO unlabeled compression of size 2. The unlabeled analogue of the compression conjecture is FALSE. TYPE IMPACT: Labeled compression schemes are strictly more powerful than unlabeled. Labels carry irreducible information for reconstruction.
nodes.formal_definition: Unlabeled compression scheme of size k: compression retains only point set S'⊆{x₁,...,x_m} (without labels); reconstruction determines correct concept from point locations alone.
key_theorems: TYPE IMPACT: Labeled compression schemes are strictly more powerful than unlabeled
open_problems: The unlabeled analogue of the compression conjecture is FALSE

provenance.note: Positive results by Kuzmin-Warmuth. Negative result (impossibility) by Pálvölgyi-Tardos 2020.

nodes.name: Online vs PAC vs Gold Separation
nodes.description: SEPARATIONS (all unconditional): (1) PAC-learnable but NOT online-learnable: thresholds on ℝ (VCdim=1, Ldim=∞). (2) Gold-identifiable but NOT PAC-learnable: all finite subsets of ℕ (identifiable from text, VCdim=∞). (3) PAC-learnable but NOT Gold-identifiable from text: superfinite classes with finite VC dimension. HIERARCHY: Gold-informant (any countable class) ⊋ PAC (finite VCdim) ⊋ Online finite-mistakes (finite Ldim) ≈ Private PAC (Alon et al. 2019) ⊆ Exact via EQ+MQ (Angluin 1988). The DP-PAC ↔ Online equivalence is the deepest bridge: Ldim < ∞ ↔ privately PAC learnable.
key_theorems: HIERARCHY: Gold-informant (any countable class) ⊋ PAC (finite VCdim) ⊋ Online finite-mistakes (finite Ldim) ≈ Private PAC (Alon et al
The DP-PAC ↔ Online equivalence is the deepest bridge: Ldim < ∞ ↔ privately PAC learnable
nodes.formal_definition: Three strict unconditional separations: (1) PAC ↛ Online: thresholds on ℝ (VCdim=1, Ldim=∞). (2) Gold-text ↛ PAC: all finite subsets of ℕ (identifiable, VCdim=∞). (3) Online ⟹ PAC (finite Ldim ⟹ finite VCdim). Hierarchy: Gold-informant ⊋ PAC ⊋ Online(finite-mistakes) ≈ DP-PAC.

provenance.note: Gold 1967 established the framework. Individual separations proved across 1967-2019.

nodes.name: Covering Number / Metric Entropy
nodes.description: DEFINITION: N(ε, H, d) = min{|V| : V ⊆ H, ∀h∈H ∃v∈V with d(h,v) ≤ ε}. Metric entropy = log N(ε, H, d). ROLE: Enters Dudley's entropy integral for Rademacher complexity: R_n(H) ≤ inf_{α≥0} (4α + 12/√n · ∫_α^∞ √(log N(ε, H, L₂)) dε). Also used in Zhang et al. 2026. CONNECTION TO VC: For binary H with VCdim=d: log N(ε, H, L₁(P)) ≤ d·log(2e/ε) for any distribution P. This bridges VC dimension to metric entropy. DATA-DEPENDENT: Empirical covering numbers condition on sample, yielding tighter bounds than worst-case.
nodes.formal_definition: N(ε, H, d) = min{|V| : V ⊆ H, ∀h∈H ∃v∈V with d(h,v) ≤ ε}. Metric entropy = log N(ε, H, d).
key_theorems: DATA-DEPENDENT: Empirical covering numbers condition on sample, yielding tighter bounds than worst-case
connections: CONNECTION TO VC: For binary H with VCdim=d: log N(ε, H, L₁(P)) ≤ d·log(2e/ε) for any distribution P
This bridges VC dimension to metric entropy

nodes.name: Pseudodimension
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Pollard 1984): For F ⊆ [0,1]^X, F P-shatters set {x₁,...,x_n} if ∃ thresholds s₁,...,s_n such that for ALL subsets E ⊆ {x₁,...,x_n}, ∃ f_E ∈ F with f_E(x_i) ≥ s_i for x_i ∈ E and f_E(x_i) < s_i for x_i ∉ E. Pdim(F) = max size of P-shattered set. RELATION TO VC: For binary-valued F, Pdim = VCdim. For real-valued F, Pdim generalizes VCdim by introducing witness thresholds. For linear functions in R^d: Pdim = d+1. ROLE: Bounds covering numbers and uniform convergence for real-valued function classes. Not scale-sensitive (unlike fat-shattering).
nodes.formal_definition: Pdim(F) = max{n : ∃ {x₁,...,x_n} ⊂ X, ∃ (s₁,...,s_n) ∈ ℝⁿ s.t. ∀ E ⊆ [n], ∃ f ∈ F with f(xᵢ) ≥ sᵢ ∀i∈E and f(xᵢ) < sᵢ ∀i∉E}
key_theorems: For linear functions ℝ^d → ℝ: Pdim = d+1
Pdim(F) < ∞ ⟹ N(ε,F,L₁(P)) ≤ (2e/ε)^Pdim for all P
nodes.scope_note: Real-valued extension of VCdim. Included for completeness beyond binary kernel.

nodes.name: Fat-Shattering Dimension
nodes.description: DEFINITION: For F ⊆ [0,1]^X and margin γ > 0, F γ-shatters {x₁,...,x_n} if ∃ thresholds s₁,...,s_n such that ∀ E ⊆ [n], ∃ f ∈ F with f(xᵢ) ≥ sᵢ+γ ∀i∈E and f(xᵢ) ≤ sᵢ-γ ∀i∉E. fat_γ(F) = max size of γ-shattered set. SCALE-SENSITIVE: Unlike Pdim, fat_γ depends on margin γ. As γ→0, fat_γ → Pdim. CHARACTERIZATION: F is PAC-learnable (real-valued) iff fat_γ(F) < ∞ for all γ > 0. CONNECTION TO COVERING: log N(ε,F,L₂) = O(fat_{ε/4}(F) · log²(1/ε)). ROLE: The real-valued analog of VCdim. Characterizes learnability for regression/real-valued prediction.
nodes.formal_definition: fat_γ(F) = max{n : ∃ {x₁,...,x_n}, ∃ (s₁,...,s_n) s.t. ∀ E ⊆ [n], ∃ f ∈ F with f(xᵢ) ≥ sᵢ+γ ∀i∈E, f(xᵢ) ≤ sᵢ-γ ∀i∉E}
key_theorems: F real-valued PAC learnable ↔ fat_γ(F) < ∞ for all γ > 0 (Alon et al. 1997)
log N₂(ε,F,x₁ⁿ) ≤ O(fat_{ε/4} · log²(n/fat_{ε/4}))
nodes.scope_note: Scale-sensitive real-valued extension of VCdim. Included for completeness beyond binary kernel.

provenance.note: Kearns-Schapire 1994 for the dimension; Alon et al. 1997 for the characterization

nodes.name: Natarajan Dimension
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Natarajan 1989): For H ⊆ Y^X with |Y| > 2, S = {x₁,...,x_n} is N-shattered by H if ∃ f₀,f₁ : S → Y with f₀(xᵢ) ≠ f₁(xᵢ) ∀i, and ∀ T ⊆ S, ∃ h ∈ H with h(xᵢ) = f₀(xᵢ) ∀xᵢ∈T, h(xᵢ) = f₁(xᵢ) ∀xᵢ∉T. d_N(H) = max |S| that is N-shattered. EQUALS VC dim when |Y|=2. BOUNDS (Ben-David et al. 1995): C₁·d_N/ε ≤ m_PAC ≤ C₂·d_N·ln(k)·ln(1/ε)/ε where k=|Y|. DOES NOT CHARACTERIZE multiclass PAC learnability for |Y|=∞: Brukhim et al. 2022 construct non-learnable class with d_N = 1. DS DIMENSION (Daniely-Shalev-Shwartz 2014) is the correct characterization.
nodes.formal_definition: d_N(H) = max{|S| : ∃ f₀,f₁:S→Y with f₀≠f₁ pointwise, and ∀T⊆S ∃h∈H agreeing with f₀ on T and f₁ on S\T}
key_theorems: Finite d_N necessary but NOT sufficient for multiclass PAC learnability when |Y| = ∞ (Brukhim et al. 2022)
DS dimension characterizes multiclass PAC learnability (Brukhim et al. 2022)
open_problems: Tight multiclass sample complexity without log(k) factor
nodes.scope_note: Multiclass extension of VCdim. Included because transformers are fundamentally multiclass (next-token over vocabulary).

nodes.name: DS Dimension (Daniely-Shalev-Shwartz)
nodes.description: DEFINITION (Daniely-Shalev-Shwartz 2014): Based on pseudo-cubes in one-inclusion hypergraphs. A class H has DS dim d if d is the largest dimension of a finite pseudo-cube in the one-inclusion hypergraph of H. CHARACTERIZATION (Brukhim et al. 2022): Multiclass PAC learnable ↔ finite DS dimension. This resolved a 30+ year open problem. KEY DISTINCTION FROM NATARAJAN: d_N can be 1 while DS dim is infinite (exotic construction via hyperbolic pseudo-manifolds). DS dim always ≥ d_N. For finite |Y|: both characterize learnability (up to log factors). For infinite |Y|: only DS dim works.
nodes.formal_definition: DS(H) = max{d : ∃ finite d-dimensional pseudo-cube in OIG(H)}
key_theorems: Finite DS dim ↔ multiclass PAC learnable (Brukhim et al. 2022)
∃ class with d_N = 1 but DS dim = ∞ (non-learnable)
open_problems: Tight quantitative bounds for DS-based sample complexity
nodes.scope_note: The correct multiclass characterization. Critical for transformer analysis (vocabulary = label space).

nodes.name: Margin-Based Generalization
nodes.description: DEFINITION: For classifier h with real-valued outputs, the margin on (x,y) is γ(x,y) = y·h(x) (for binary y∈{±1}). Larger margin = more confident. MARGIN-BASED BOUND (Bartlett 1998): Generalization error ≤ empirical margin loss + O(R_n(F_γ)/γ) where F_γ is the function class scaled to margin γ. KEY INSIGHT: A class can have infinite VCdim but finite fat-shattering dimension at relevant margins, yielding meaningful bounds. This explains why SVMs and neural nets generalize despite infinite VC dimension. BARTLETT-FOSTER-TELGARSKY 2017: Spectrally-normalized margin bounds for deep networks. Product of spectral norms × margin controls generalization.
nodes.formal_definition: margin(h,x,y) = y·h(x). Margin bound: P[y≠sign(h(x))] ≤ P̂[y·h(x) ≤ γ] + O(R_n(F)/γ + √(ln(1/δ)/n))
key_theorems: Generalization ≤ empirical margin loss + Rademacher complexity at scale γ (Bartlett 1998)
For deep nets: gen bound ∝ (∏ᵢ‖Wᵢ‖_σ)²/(γ²n) (Bartlett-Foster-Telgarsky 2017)
open_problems: Tighter bounds for practical deep nets
Whether spectral norm product captures true complexity
nodes.scope_note: Bridges VCdim (infinite for neural nets) with practical generalization. Essential for transformer analysis.

nodes.name: [Scope Boundary] Multi-Armed Bandits
nodes.description: NOT IN SCOPE. Multi-armed bandits (EXP3, UCB) are an optimization/exploration framework, not classification. Sample complexity results exist (e.g., PAC bounds for best-arm identification) but the problem structure (reward maximization under partial feedback) differs fundamentally from concept learning. Included as boundary marker.
nodes.formal_definition: Bandit problem: at each round t, choose arm a_t ∈ [K], observe reward r_t(a_t) only. Regret = Σ(r*(t) - r_t(a_t)). NOT a classification problem.

provenance.field_origin: Scope boundary marker. See Auer et al. 2002 for the framework.

nodes.scope_note: Excluded: different problem structure (reward maximization, not concept identification). PAC-bandit connections exist but are application-level.

nodes.name: [Scope Boundary] Reinforcement Learning
nodes.description: NOT IN SCOPE. RL extends bandits to sequential decision-making with state transitions. PAC-MDP bounds exist (Strehl et al. 2006) but the problem structure (MDP, policy, value function) is fundamentally different from concept learning. Eluder dimension (Russo-Van Roy 2013) provides a bridge but the full RL framework is out of scope.
nodes.formal_definition: MDP = (S, A, P, R, γ). Policy π: S → A. PAC-MDP: find ε-optimal policy with prob ≥ 1-δ using poly(|S|,|A|,1/ε,1/δ,1/(1-γ)) samples.

provenance.field_origin: Scope boundary marker.

nodes.scope_note: Excluded: different problem structure (sequential decision-making, not concept identification).

nodes.name: [Scope Boundary] Quantum Learning Theory
nodes.description: NOT IN SCOPE. Quantum PAC learning, quantum query complexity, and quantum SQ models exist but require quantum state/measurement formalism beyond classical learning theory. Key results: quantum speedup for certain concept classes (Bshouty-Jackson 1998), quantum-classical sample complexity gaps. The no-signaling constraint on learnability (arXiv 2601.12651, 2026) is a recent development.
nodes.formal_definition: Quantum PAC: learner receives quantum examples |ψ_x,c(x)⟩ = Σ√D(x)|x,c(x)⟩. Sample complexity can differ from classical by polynomial factors.

provenance.field_origin: Scope boundary marker.

nodes.scope_note: Excluded: requires quantum formalism. Classical results subsume for classical data.

Both bound generalization via algorithm properties. Stability uses sup-norm perturbation sensitivity. Info-theory uses mutual information. Connected via the chain: low stability → low I(W;S) → low generalization gap, but the proof methods are fundamentally different.
Granger tests causal structure (does past X improve Y prediction?). Online learning minimizes cumulative prediction error. Different objectives: causal inference vs prediction optimization.

edges.note: Computational hardness theorem states: finite VCdim does NOT imply efficient PAC learning under crypto assumptions. VCdim appears in the premise, not as a quantity being bounded.

edges.obstruction: SQdim measures pairwise correlation structure under a distribution. VCdim measures combinatorial shattering capacity (distribution-free). Exponential gap possible (parities: VCdim=n, SQdim=2^n). They measure fundamentally different properties.

edges.note: Forward direction (Occam implies PAC) is general. Converse (PAC implies Occam) holds only for polynomially closed concept classes (Board-Pitt 1993). See also BoardPitt1992.

edges.obstruction: Occam uses description length of hypothesis. Compression uses subset of training data. Both embody "short representation generalizes" but the notion of shortness differs: syntactic (bits to encode h) vs extensional (training examples to reconstruct h).

Occam is a PAC generalization theorem (short hypothesis → low error). MDL is an information-theoretic model selection principle (minimize total description). Structurally parallel but Occam gives worst-case bounds while MDL gives Bayesian/information-theoretic guarantees.
